Switchbacks characterize this section as the route gains elevation rapidly. From Gunsight Pass, the route continues east toward Anderson Pass at 12,700 to 12,800 feet, which serves as the final approach point before the summit push. The crux of the climb is the final ridge section from Anderson Pass to the summit, roughly 800 vertical feet of off-trail boulder hopping and scrambling. Once on the ridge, stay to the ridgeline and you will reach the summit cairn. This section is exposed to weather and wind, and parties navigate open talus and rock.
permitsClimbing: No climbing permit is required for Kings Peak. Day hikers and overnight parties register at a free sign-in kiosk located at the Henry's Fork Trailhead. The peak lies within the High Uintas Wilderness and falls under the jurisdiction of Ashley National Forest.
permitsCamping: No advance permits are required for backcountry camping. The Henry's Fork Campground sits at the trailhead with no reservations. On-route campsites include Dollar Lake at 7 to 8 miles and 11,000 feet, the most popular basecamp with established sites on the north and west sides. Alligator Lake at 3 miles and Henry's Lake in the basin offer earlier camping options. Upper basin campsites above Dollar Lake are possible for parties seeking higher altitude camps. Backcountry camping is generally permitted throughout the High Uintas Wilderness, subject to current Forest Service regulations. Establish camps at least 100 feet from water sources. Fires are restricted in high-use areas including the Dollar Lake basin; check current Forest Service regulations before your trip.
wildlife: Moose are regularly spotted around Henry's Fork Lake and Dollar Lake, particularly from June through August; maintain at least 75 feet (25 meters) distance if encountered. Elk are regularly spotted in the Upper Yellowstone Creek Basin. Alpine zones support marmots and pikas in the talus fields. Mosquitoes are heavy around lakes during mid-summer months.

overview: Mount Graham ranks No. 20 among the 57 ultra-prominent peaks in the contiguous United States, rising 10,709 feet (LiDAR; traditionally listed at 10,720 feet) in the Pinaleno Mountains of Arizona with 6,313 feet of clean prominence. It is the highest point in Graham County and the most prominent peak in Arizona, but its summit lies within the Mount Graham Red Squirrel Refugium. Access is shaped by closure of the High Peak Road summit corridor, where the usual route covers about 8 to 9 miles round-trip with 1,600 feet of gain on Class 1 terrain but public summit entry requires special authorization.

The peak holds significance in Western Apache tradition as Dził Nchaa Sí'an, meaning "Big Seated Mountain." Mount Graham's high point lies within the Mount Graham Red Squirrel Refugium, a federal habitat protection area established to protect an endangered subspecies found nowhere else on Earth. This designation creates a unique and legally complex situation for peak-baggers: the summit is formally closed to public access, yet many reported ascents appear in public peak-bagging registries.
routeDescription: That split between physical simplicity and legal closure is what makes Mount Graham so contentious. Public peak-bagging logs show that many climbers have reached the summit anyway, while others stop at the boundary, count a nearby substitute, take the observatory tour, or leave the peak unfinished. None of those choices is universally accepted by the ultra-prominence community.

The honest description is this: Mount Graham is not hard because of terrain. It is hard because the summit is closed. For peak-baggers, Mount Graham is ultimately an ethics-and-risk decision rather than a routefinding challenge. The physical line is straightforward, but the access problem is real. That is why the peak remains one of the most debated summits on the lower-48 ultra list.

Hikers who want a legal high-country objective in the Pinaleño Mountains commonly look to Webb Peak (10,029 ft), Heliograph Peak (10,028 ft), and Clark Peak, all accessible via the Swift Trail and associated established routes. These summits do not replace Mount Graham for all ultra-prominence list requirements, but they offer straightforward access to the range's highest elevations and excellent views without entering the restricted summit area.
permitsClimbing: Summit access is restricted within the Mount Graham Red Squirrel Refugium; special authorization is required for legal entry. Eastern Arizona College operates an educational tour program through the Mount Graham International Observatory that provides vehicle access and views from near the facility. The tour operates seasonally (typically mid-May through October). Fees and availability vary by season and should be confirmed with the tour operator.
permitsCamping: State Route 366 (Swift Trail Parkway) reopens approximately mid-April (typically April 15 per AZDOT) and closes again before winter, typically around November 15. Verify current road status at az511.gov before travel.

Coronado National Forest allows dispersed camping in many undeveloped areas outside the restricted zone, subject to posted closures, wilderness rules, fire restrictions, and Leave No Trace practices. Shannon Campground is located near 9,100 feet on the upper Swift Trail Parkway. Roper Lake State Park at the base offers alternative facilities and camping.
wildlife: Black bears inhabit the Pinaleño Mountains, so campers should use proper food storage and keep a clean camp. The peak's elevation above 10,000 feet requires basic acclimatization; rapid ascent from the hot base can stress unprepared hikers. Afternoon thunderstorms are common July through August and warrant early starts and timing discipline. The remote location demands self-sufficiency and solid logistics planning.
footnote: Summit N 32.70167° W 109.87141°. Swift Trail Parkway (State Route 366) access typically open mid-April to mid-November; verify closure points at az511.gov. Elevation 10,709 ft (LiDAR); prominence 6,313 ft. Coronado National Forest, Graham County.

overview: Mount San Antonio ranks No. 21 among the 57 ultra-prominent peaks in the contiguous United States, rising 10,064 feet in the San Gabriel Mountains of Southern California, with 6,247 feet of clean prominence. It is the highest summit in the San Gabriel Mountains and the high point of Los Angeles County on the Los Angeles–San Bernardino county boundary. Most hikers use the Devil's Backbone from Manker Flats for roughly 10 to 11 miles round-trip with about 3,900 to 4,300 feet of gain on Class 1–2 terrain with sustained ridge exposure. Parking at Manker Flats requires a day-use fee or valid federal recreation pass.

More commonly called Mount Baldy, the peak is visible across much of the Los Angeles Basin, where its snow-capped profile appears above the horizon on clear winter and spring mornings. The summit lies roughly 35 miles northeast of downtown Los Angeles within Angeles National Forest.

The San Gabriels are a tectonically active range uplifting rapidly along the San Andreas and related fault systems. Baldy's upper slopes are composed of granitic rock characteristic of the Transverse Ranges. The Devil's Backbone, which forms the final section of the standard route, is a narrow crest shaped by frost action and mass wasting, with steep drops on both sides. The Sierra Club Ski Hut at approximately 8,200 feet on the Baldy Bowl approach has served climbers and skiers for decades and is reservable through the Sierra Club Angeles Chapter.
routeDescription: The standard route begins at Manker Flats Trailhead at approximately 6,160 feet and follows the road or trail up to Baldy Notch before continuing along the Devil's Backbone ridge to the summit. From Manker Flats, the full outing commonly runs 10 to 11 miles round-trip with roughly 3,900 to 4,300 feet of gain; parties using the Mount Baldy Resort ski lift to Baldy Notch substantially reduce both. From the notch at approximately 7,800 feet, the Backbone Trail traverses a narrow exposed crest northeast toward the summit, with steep drops on both sides in sections. In dry conditions the ridge is Class 1–2 hiking with exposure; wind, snow, or ice can make the terrain significantly more serious. Most parties allow 6 to 10 hours round-trip from Manker Flats.

Other established routes include the Baldy Bowl Trail from Manker Flats via the Sierra Club Ski Hut, a steeper alternative with a sustained scree section, and the longer Icehouse Canyon approach from the east.
permitsClimbing: Parking at Manker Flats requires payment of a day-use fee or a valid federal recreation pass; the Adventure Pass has historically been the applicable instrument for this area, but the Manker Flat day-use fee and pass rules are subject to change. Day-hiking the Devil's Backbone route generally does not require a wilderness permit, but wilderness permits are required for entry into designated wilderness areas along the route and are free. Confirm current parking, pass, and permit requirements directly with Angeles National Forest before departure.
permitsCamping: Backcountry camping rules vary by wilderness area and management unit around Mount Baldy; check current Angeles National Forest and San Bernardino National Forest guidance before planning an overnight trip. A California campfire permit may be required for stoves or open flames outside developed facilities, and seasonal fire restrictions frequently prohibit all flames. The Sierra Club Ski Hut at 8,200 feet is reservable through the Sierra Club Angeles Chapter and provides a shelter option for parties making an early start. Water may be available near Manker Flats or developed facilities; do not rely on untreated backcountry sources.
wildlife: Comfort on exposed Class 2 ridge terrain is required in dry summer conditions. Snow or ice above Baldy Notch requires self-arrest competency with ice axe and crampons, and winter and spring conditions can persist on the upper route well into May. Rockfall from parties above in Baldy Bowl is a hazard on that approach; the Devil's Backbone route reduces this exposure. The exposed summit ridge carries serious consequence in wind or icy conditions. Trailhead parking at Manker Flats fills by early morning on peak-season weekends.
footnote: Summit N 34.28940° W 117.64640°. Manker Flats Trailhead: N 34.23320° W 117.64660° (~6,160 ft). Elevation 10,064 ft; prominence 6,247 ft. Angeles National Forest, San Gabriel Mountains.

overview: Telescope Peak ranks No. 22 among the 57 ultra-prominent peaks in the contiguous United States, rising 11,049 feet in the Panamint Mountains of California with 6,168 feet of clean prominence. It is the highest point in Death Valley National Park, with more than 11,300 feet of local relief above Badwater Basin. Most hikers use the Mahogany Flat Campground Trail for 14 miles round-trip with 3,000 feet of gain on Class 1 terrain.

The mountain's upper slopes support ancient bristlecone pines, some more than 3,000 years old, while the lower elevations transition quickly into desert scrub. This sharp ecological gradient is one of the defining features of the climb. The Wildrose Charcoal Kilns, ten stone structures built in 1877 for mining operations, stand near the trailhead as a notable historical stop.
routeDescription: The standard route begins at Mahogany Flat Campground (8,133 feet) and follows a maintained National Park Service trail for approximately 13–14 miles round-trip with 3,000 feet of elevation gain. The trail climbs steadily to the main ridge, passes the Bennett Peak area and Arcane Meadows, then continues along a broad open ridgeline before steepening through switchbacks across exposed alpine terrain to the 11,049-foot summit. In dry conditions, the route is Class 1 hiking with clear tread and straightforward navigation; the main challenges are distance, altitude, sun exposure, wind, and lack of shelter along the upper ridge.

The Hanaupah Canyon approach from the Death Valley side, starting near Shorty's Well, gains approximately 11,300 feet over roughly 30 miles round-trip. This route is rarely used and recommended only for experienced desert mountaineers with extensive water management and logistics planning.
routeLandmarks: Bennett Peak area / ridge high point (near 10,000 ft)
permitsClimbing: No permit is required for a day hike of Telescope Peak. For overnight backcountry trips, free voluntary backcountry permits are available for Telescope Peak; self-registered permits can be submitted online or at ranger stations. Permits for other popular Death Valley backcountry areas require advance reservation through Recreation.gov (up to six months ahead) and carry a $10 per permit fee; check current National Park Service rules to confirm whether Telescope Peak's wilderness area falls under the reservation system.
permitsCamping: Mahogany Flat Campground is a free, first-come, first-served facility open seasonally (generally March through November depending on snow and road conditions). Pit toilets are available, but there is no running water; pack all water in. Thorndike Campground (6 sites, 2 miles downcanyon) and Wildrose Campground (23 sites, 9 miles away, water available year-round) serve as alternatives. Bear canisters are not a normal requirement for this arid high-desert environment, but all food, trash, and scented items must be secured from rodents, ravens, coyotes, and other wildlife. Fire regulations vary by season and area; verify current National Park Service rules before planning an overnight trip.
wildlife: In dry summer conditions, the standard route requires no specialized climbing skills. Shoulder-season and winter ascents may involve snow and ice requiring traction, route-finding judgment, and appropriate snow-travel experience. The exposed alpine ridge offers strong wind and minimal shelter, with rapid weather changes and occasional summer thunderstorms. Lightning risk is significant on the open summit and ridgeline, and the summit is often several dozen degrees cooler than the valley floor. The unmaintained dirt road to Mahogany Flat is commonly recommended for high-clearance vehicles and may close or become impassable after snow, storms, or washouts. Late June through October is typically the most reliable snow-free window; shoulder-season ascents in May or November may involve snow or ice on the upper ridge. Bennett Peak is a natural short side trip from the main ridge; Rogers Peak is also accessible from the broader ridge system but requires significantly more time and planning.
footnote: Summit 36.16984°N, 117.08921°W. Mahogany Flat Campground N 36.2073° W 117.0967° (~8,133 ft). Elevation 11,049 ft; prominence 6,168 ft. Death Valley National Park.

overview: Mount Peale ranks No. 23 among the 57 ultra-prominent peaks in the contiguous United States, rising 12,727 feet in the La Sal Mountains of eastern Utah, with 6,180 feet of clean prominence. It is the highest summit in the La Sal Mountains and San Juan County. Most hikers use the Burlfriends Trail from La Sal Pass for 5 to 6 miles round-trip with about 2,600 feet of gain on Class 2 terrain with off-trail navigation above treeline.

The La Sals contain Utah's highest summits outside the Uinta Mountains, and on ListsOfJohn, Peale ranks second behind Kings Peak among Utah peaks by prominence. The range rises sharply above the canyon country around Moab, creating a direct transition from the red-rock Colorado Plateau to alpine terrain within a few vertical miles. From Moab, Mount Tukuhnikivatz often appears higher on the skyline than Peale, though it stands roughly 250 feet lower, a reliable source of confusion for observers approaching from the west.

The La Sal Mountains are a classic laccolithic range, formed when igneous intrusions pushed overlying sedimentary rock upward without reaching the surface as lava. Above treeline, the terrain gives way to loose talus fields with minimal vegetation and a high-alpine character that can feel remote despite the range's proximity to Moab. On clear days the summit provides views extending to Arches National Park, Canyonlands National Park, and ranges more than 100 miles distant.
routeDescription: The standard approach begins at La Sal Pass Trailhead at approximately 10,125 feet, following the Burlfriends Trail south through grassy meadows and open conifer forest. The maintained trail gives way to an off-trail route at the base of the upper gully; above that point there is no constructed summit trail, and parties follow use paths and boot tracks through loose talus toward the ridge. The round trip from the pass trailhead is 5 to 6 miles with approximately 2,600 feet of gain, typically requiring 5 to 7 hours. Navigation requires attention on the upper mountain, especially in poor visibility.

In favorable conditions, high-clearance 4x4 vehicles may be able to continue farther along the approach road, shortening the hike; road conditions vary significantly with snow, mud, runoff damage, and seasonal closures, and the La Sal Pass Road includes a creek crossing that requires higher clearance.
routeLandmarks: Upper gully entry / off-trail transition point
permitsClimbing: No permit is required to climb Mount Peale or access La Sal Pass in Manti-La Sal National Forest, and no trailhead quota or self-registration applies.
permitsCamping: Dispersed camping is generally allowed on national forest land in the area; stay limits cap any single site at 14 days within any 60-day period, after which campers must move at least 10 miles. Dispersed camping is not permitted within developed trailhead or picnic areas. Fire restrictions, road closures, and seasonal rules apply; confirm current conditions with the Moab Ranger District before any overnight trip. Water is scarce at and above treeline, so plan procurement carefully on multi-day itineraries.
wildlife: The standard route is Class 2, with off-trail navigation required above the lower trail and occasional Class 2+ movement on loose talus near the upper gully and summit ridge. No rope or harness is needed under summer conditions. Acclimatization is a real factor: the trailhead sits above 10,000 feet and the summit above 12,700 feet, with the full elevation gain compressed into 2.5 to 3 miles. For early-season ascents, crampons and an ice axe may be required if firm snow remains in shaded gully sections or on north-facing slopes. Afternoon thunderstorms are possible July through August. Wind exposure on the summit ridge is significant, and the range sees low enough visitation that self-rescue planning is appropriate.
footnote: Summit N 38.43880° W 109.22940°. La Sal Pass Trailhead: N 38.41943° W 109.25206° (approximately 10,125–10,134 ft). Elevation 12,727 ft; prominence 6,180 ft. Manti-La Sal National Forest, La Sal Mountains.

overview: Mount Washington ranks No. 24 among the 57 ultra-prominent peaks in the contiguous United States, rising 6,288 feet in the White Mountains of New Hampshire with 6,148 feet of clean prominence. It is the highest point in the Northeastern United States. Most hikers use the Tuckerman Ravine Trail for roughly 8.2 to 8.6 miles round-trip with about 4,100 to 4,300 feet of gain, finishing on exposed above-treeline terrain. The summit plateau is exposed to severe wind and rapid weather changes in all seasons.

Geologically, Mount Washington consists of quartzite and mica schist bedrock exposed above treeline, creating a barren, windswept summit plateau. The Appalachian Trail crosses the summit, making Mount Washington one of the most prominent and weather-exposed summits on the AT corridor. Despite its modest absolute elevation, Mount Washington presents hazards comparable to far higher peaks due to its latitude, exposure, and weather patterns. The Sherman Adams summit building houses a museum, cafeteria, and meteorological observatory—infrastructure that makes the summit unusually developed compared with most major hiking peaks.
routeDescription: The Tuckerman Ravine Trail is a strenuous non-technical hike, best described as Class 1+ in normal summer conditions, with steep rock steps and boulder-strewn footing near the ravine and summit cone. The first 2.4 miles ascend through forest to Hermit Lake shelters (approximately 1,800 feet of gain), establishing a steady grade on well-maintained trail. From Hermit Lake, the route steepens as it enters Tuckerman Ravine itself, a classic glacial cirque bounded by steep headwalls. The final approach to the summit cone involves rock-hopping over angular boulders, with the steepest pitches occurring in the final few hundred feet. Total round-trip distance is 8.2–8.6 miles; experienced parties typically complete this in 5–10 hours depending on conditions and fitness.

Other established approaches include the Lion Head Trail (steeper and rougher, approximately 3.6 miles to summit), the Ammonoosuc Ravine Trail (a shorter but steep and rugged west-side approach), and the Huntington Ravine Trail (involving exposed scrambling, appropriate only for experienced hikers with existing scrambling competency). The summit is also accessible via the Mount Washington Cog Railway and Auto Road—an alternative to hiking for parties wishing to avoid the ascent.
routeLandmarks: Hermit Lake Shelters (approximately 1,800 ft gain, ~2.4 miles from Pinkham Notch)
Tuckerman Ravine floor/headwall area — seasonal snow, ice, avalanche, and closure concerns
Summit cone — rough rock steps and boulder-strewn trail above treeline
permitsClimbing: No special climbing permit is required to ascend Mount Washington. Parking, recreation-pass requirements, and facility rules vary by trailhead and managing organization, including White Mountain National Forest and AMC-operated facilities in the area. Verify current fees and pass requirements before departure. Services at Pinkham Notch Visitor Center and on the summit vary seasonally; verify current hours before relying on food, water, restrooms, or indoor shelter.
permitsCamping: Hermit Lake Shelters provide managed backcountry camping below Tuckerman Ravine, with lean-tos, tent platforms, outhouses, seasonal water access, and caretaker-managed procedures. Individual campers purchase permits at AMC Pinkham Notch Visitor Center; confirm current booking procedures and stay limits before planning. The AMC Lakes of the Clouds Hut (5,012 feet) offers seasonal bunk accommodations with meals; reservations required during full-service season. The Harvard Cabin operates as a winter facility with limited cabin and tent-site accommodations; confirm current dates and procedures before planning. Above-treeline camping is generally prohibited. The winter exception applies only when at least two feet of snow covers the ground, and it does not allow camping in the Cutler River Drainage, including Tuckerman and Huntington Ravines.
wildlife: The Tuckerman Ravine Trail is non-technical with relentless elevation gain and a sustained moderate-to-steep grade. The route remains a trail hike in normal summer conditions, but the summit cone and upper ravine sections involve rough footing, rock steps, and boulder travel. Alternative routes vary significantly in difficulty: Lion Head Trail is steeper and rougher, while Huntington Ravine Trail involves exposed scrambling and is appropriate only for experienced hikers with existing scrambling competency. Mount Washington's weather is the primary hazard. The peak is known colloquially as the "Home of the World's Worst Weather" and experiences severe storms, including snow and sleet, during any month of the year. Winds can exceed 60 mph even in summer, and the combination of wind, cold, and wetness can exhaust strong hikers. More than 100 deaths have been documented on and around Mount Washington, with some counts approaching or exceeding 150 depending on the area and date range included. A rapid deterioration of conditions at higher elevations can leave parties exposed and unable to descend safely. Parties should carry adequate insulation and rain protection regardless of forecast conditions and be prepared to turn back if conditions worsen.
footnote: Elevation 6,288 ft (traditional public figure); 6,286.5 ft NAVD88 may appear in technical sources. Prominence approximately 6,148 ft. Wind record: 231 mph, April 12, 1934. Parking, recreation-pass, and facility fees vary by trailhead; verify current AMC, WMNF, and MWAC requirements before travel.

overview: Mount Mitchell ranks No. 25 among the 57 ultra-prominent peaks in the contiguous United States, rising 6,684 feet in the Black Mountains of North Carolina with 6,090 feet of clean prominence. It is the highest peak east of the Mississippi River within Mount Mitchell State Park. Most hikers use the Mount Mitchell Trail for roughly 11.2 to 12 miles round-trip with about 3,600 to 3,700 feet of gain on Class 1 terrain.

The summit marks a distinct ecological boundary. Visiting hikers encounter conditions characteristic of far northern forests despite the latitude, with winter snowfall averaging 104 inches and recorded temperatures dropping to −34°F. Dense fog, cold rain, and strong winds can occur in any season, making this an unusually severe mountain environment for the eastern slope of the Appalachians.
routeDescription: The Mount Mitchell Trail is the standard and most heavily traveled non-motorized route to the summit, ascending from Black Mountain Campground. The trail gains approximately 3,600 to 3,700 feet over 11.2 to 12 miles round-trip on sustained but well-maintained footpath. The route begins near Black Mountain Campground at roughly 2,995 feet, follows gentle grades through deciduous forest in the lower sections, then steepens as switchbacks climb toward the high ridge. The physical crux is the relentless sustained climbing with minimal rest sections, particularly in the upper reaches where the trail enters the dense spruce-fir forest. Above 5,000 feet, fog and wind exposure increase markedly, and travel can feel significantly more severe than lower sections despite remaining on trail. The route finishes at the developed summit area, where a short path leads to the observation deck.
The Black Mountain Crest Trail offers a significantly more strenuous alternative, traversing the high ridge over terrain requiring strong fitness and comfort with steep, rugged footing.
permitsClimbing: No climbing permit is required for day hiking Mount Mitchell. Mount Mitchell State Park charges no day-use entrance fee for vehicles parking outside the summit area. However, the Mount Mitchell State Park campground at Black Mountain Campground is closed for the entire 2025 and 2026 seasons due to water line installation. Verify current access and campground status before planning an overnight trip, as operations are subject to seasonal closures and maintenance.
permitsCamping: The Mount Mitchell State Park campground at Black Mountain Campground is currently closed for the entire 2025 and 2026 seasons due to water line installation and will reopen in 2027. When the campground operates in future years, non-electric tent camping costs $27 per night and requires advance reservation through Reserve America. Backcountry camping rules vary by jurisdiction; confirm current registration or permit requirements with the relevant land manager before departure. Water is available from springs in the lower sections of the trail during normal conditions. Upper-elevation water sources are unreliable; parties should carry sufficient water from lower elevations. Fire regulations vary by location and season; confirm current requirements with the park office before camping. Black bears are present in the region; inquire at the visitor center regarding current food storage protocols and bear-awareness guidelines.
wildlife: Peak season runs May through October. Winter snowfall averages 104 inches at the summit, with recorded temperatures dropping to −34°F. Dense fog, cold rain, and strong winds can occur in any season, making this an unusually severe mountain environment for the eastern slope of the Appalachians despite the moderate summit elevation.
footnote: Stats sources: USGS and NC State Parks (elevation 6,684 ft); ListsOfJohn (prominence 6,090 ft); NC State Parks Mount Mitchell Trail description (11.2–12 miles round-trip, 3,600–3,700 ft gain). Campground closed 2025–2026 for water line installation; reopens 2027. Future non-electric tent sites $27/night via Reserve America (rates subject to change).

overview: Humphreys Peak ranks No. 26 among the 57 ultra-prominent peaks in the contiguous United States, rising 12,633 feet in the San Francisco Peaks of Arizona with 6,042 feet of clean prominence. It is Arizona's highest summit and holds the only alpine tundra ecosystem in the state. Most hikers follow the Humphreys Peak Trail for 10 miles round-trip with 3,300 feet of gain on Class 2 terrain.

The San Francisco Peaks support several endemic plant species found nowhere else on Earth, including the San Francisco Peaks groundsel, concentrated in the tundra zone above 11,400 feet. That fragility has produced strict rules against off-trail travel and camping above treeline. The range name is generally associated with Spanish Franciscan naming traditions honoring Saint Francis of Assisi. Humphreys Peak was named for Brigadier General Andrew A. Humphreys, a 19th-century Army engineer associated with the western surveys of the period.
routeDescription: The standard route departs the Arizona Snowbowl lower parking lot at 9,333 feet and gains elevation steadily through ponderosa pine and mixed conifer forest for the first 3 miles, with switchbacks and sections of exposed tree roots and loose stone. At approximately 11,400 feet, a posted sign marks the upper camping boundary; the trail continues to Agassiz Saddle at 11,780 feet, a narrow notch separating Humphreys from Agassiz Peak to the south and the junction with the Weatherford Trail.

Above the saddle, forest gives way abruptly to open alpine terrain with wind-stunted trees, exposed rock, and low-growing tundra vegetation. The final mile to the summit traverses a series of false summits on Class 2 volcanic talus and scree, with the route fully exposed to wind and weather. The summit at 12,633 feet is marked by a wooden post and rough stone windbreak. Round-trip distance is 9.6 miles; total elevation gain is 3,300 feet.
permitsClimbing: No permit is required for day-use hiking on Humphreys Peak Trail. In winter, road access and wilderness permit requirements can vary with Arizona Snowbowl operations, storm conditions, and Forest Service decisions. Verify current road status and any permit requirements with Coconino National Forest before attempting a winter ascent.
permitsCamping: Camping above 11,400 feet is prohibited under a standing Forest Service occupancy order designed to protect the San Francisco Peaks alpine tundra ecosystem. Below that elevation, dispersed camping is permitted on Coconino National Forest land subject to current closure orders and Leave No Trace practices. The Flagstaff area has a history of fire-related closure orders that can affect dispersed camping zones on short notice. Hart Prairie, located below the San Francisco Mountain massif, is a dispersed camping area; check current Forest Service status before planning a stay. Lockett Meadow Campground, on the mountain's northeast side, is reached by a separate road and is typically seasonal with fees; road access and site availability change annually, so verify current Coconino National Forest status before relying on it. No water is available on the Humphreys Peak Trail itself; all water must be carried from the trailhead. The Snowbowl lodge at the parking area may have restrooms and water during operating hours but is not on-trail.
wildlife: Peak season runs May through October. July through September monsoon season brings rapid afternoon thunderstorm development and elevated lightning risk on the exposed upper ridge. December through May may hold snow and ice on the upper mountain, requiring traction and winter travel skills. Off-trail travel is prohibited above treeline to protect rare endemic tundra species.
footnote: Trailhead: Arizona Snowbowl lower parking lot, near 9300 N Snow Bowl Rd, Flagstaff, AZ 86001.

overview: Borah Peak ranks No. 27 among the 57 ultra-prominent peaks in the contiguous United States, rising 12,662 feet in the Lost River Range of Idaho with 5,990 feet of clean prominence. It is Idaho's highest mountain and the high point of the Lost River Range. Most hikers and scramblers ascend the Southwest Ridge for roughly 8 to 8.2 miles round-trip with about 5,200 to 5,300 feet of gain, including the exposed Class 3 Chickenout Ridge crux.

The peak is best known for Chickenout Ridge, an exposed Class 3 crux between 11,200 and 11,400 feet where the psychological exposure often exceeds the actual technical difficulty. The ridge is narrow, features loose rock, and drops steeply on the north flank. Many experienced climbers choose to turn back here despite solid route-finding skills. The October 1983 Borah Peak earthquake uplifted the Lost River fault by several feet; modern published elevations commonly cite 12,662 feet, though topographic surveys have some variation.
routeDescription: The Southwest Ridge is the standard and most-traveled route to the summit. The climb begins at Borah Peak Trailhead at 7,400 feet and gains approximately 5,200 to 5,300 feet over roughly 8 to 8.2 miles round-trip. The lower section ascends steep sagebrush-covered slopes on an established trail, reaching open alpine terrain by mid-elevation. From there, the route follows the southwest ridge spine upward through increasingly exposed scrambling terrain.

The Chickenout Ridge crux occupies the narrow section between 11,200 and 11,400 feet. This Class 3 section requires solid scrambling technique over loose rock, careful footwork, and comfort with significant exposure. The north flank drops steeply toward the basin below, creating serious exposure. Parties must navigate route variations around the ridge crest, all of which demand careful attention to foot placement and route-finding in order to avoid rockfall hazard to climbers below. Above the crux, parties continue on scree and talus slopes to reach the bare rock summit, which offers expansive views across the Lost River Range, Lemhi Mountains, and surrounding peaks to the south and east.

Descent follows the same route; the Chickenout Ridge presents the same technical challenge on the way down. Early-season snow may make glissading appealing but adds significant risk; downclimbing the Class 3 section is the safer choice.
routeLandmarks: Chickenout Ridge (11,200–11,400 ft) — exposed Class 3 scrambling with serious exposure on the north flank
Summit (12,662 ft) — bare rock with expansive alpine views
permitsClimbing: No climbing permit or quota is required for Borah Peak. Verify current road access, fire restrictions, and any temporary Forest Service closures before travel by contacting Salmon–Challis National Forest.
permitsCamping: The Borah Peak Trailhead, located 2.5 miles from Highway 93, has a primitive first-come campground and vault toilet. No reliable potable water is available at the trailhead or along the route. Parties should arrive with all water needed for the climb and trailhead camping. The steep terrain above 7,400 feet offers little practical camping; day climbing from the trailhead is standard. Dispersed camping exists nearby if the trailhead campground is full. Contact Salmon–Challis National Forest for current campground details, site availability, and seasonal access before travel.
wildlife: July through early September offers the most stable and snow-free conditions. Late June can be snow-dependent. September brings shorter days and colder conditions, with snow possible later in the month. May and June involve snow; early-season ascents require winter mountaineering competency. Downclimbing is safer than glissading on early-season snow. The sustained elevation gain of 5,200 to 5,300 feet demands strong fitness. Class 3 scrambling requires comfort with hands-and-feet movement on loose, exposed rock. Mountain navigation in poor visibility and sound turnaround discipline are essential. Ropes and climbing protection are not required in dry summer conditions. Snow-covered ascents, common in May and June and possible into July or again in September, are significantly more technical. Steep snowfields, potential avalanche terrain on the north face, and narrow snow ridges require an ice axe, crampons, and solid winter mountaineering skills with self-arrest ability. Afternoon thunderstorm development is typical July through August; the exposed ridge presents extreme lightning risk. The rapid ascent to 12,662 feet may cause altitude sickness; adequate acclimatization is important. Despite its Class 3 technical grade, Borah ranks among the most challenging state highpoints due to sustained elevation gain, altitude, exposure, and the mental discipline required to turn back if conditions warrant.
footnote: Location: Lost River Range, Idaho — Custer County; Salmon–Challis National Forest. Primary sources: Salmon–Challis National Forest, USGS GNIS, USGS Earthquake Hazards (1983 Borah Peak earthquake). Route and community sources: Idaho: A Climbing Guide, Sawtooth Mountain Guides, SummitPost, U.S. Highpoint Guide.

overview: Mount Jefferson (South Summit) ranks No. 28 among the 57 ultra-prominent peaks in the contiguous United States, rising 11,949 feet in the Toquima Range of central Nevada with 5,876 feet of clean prominence. It is the highest point in the Alta Toquima Wilderness northeast of Tonopah. Most hikers use the Jefferson Summit Access route for 4 to 6 miles round-trip with 1,500 to 2,000 feet of gain on Class 2 ridge terrain.

With approximately 100 miles of isolation, Mount Jefferson is the highest point for a vast stretch of central Nevada's high desert. The ridge traverses high alpine terrain cloaked in sparse grasses, windswept sedges, and scattered alpine shrubs. Pockets of limber pine and bristlecone pine occupy lower ridge sections and sheltered saddles. Experienced parties can traverse the ridgeline to visit multiple summits in a single day. Listed elevations vary by source and elevation model; public agency and tourism sources commonly cite roughly 11,814–11,820 feet for North Summit and 11,686–11,692 feet for Middle Summit.
routeDescription: The most direct South Summit approach begins at Jefferson Summit, approximately 8,786 feet elevation, via Forest Road 44010 (Round Mountain–Meadow Creek Road). An old forest road extends from the trailhead into the wilderness boundary, then transitions to trail and eventually cross-country terrain as elevation increases. The route gains 1,500–2,000 feet over 4–6 miles round trip, ascending steadily toward the ridge crest. The final section traces the open alpine ridge on mostly Class 1 terrain to the South Summit, though short cross-country sections may feel like easy Class 2 depending on the exact line chosen. The main challenge is not technical difficulty, but altitude, exposure, and steady elevation gain over 2 or more miles at high elevation. The outing typically takes 4–6 hours for fit parties; less experienced hikers should allow additional time and plan for turnaround well before afternoon.

The Pine Creek Trail approach is a substantially longer route that uses Pine Creek Trail to reach the Mount Jefferson Trail north of South Summit, trading the short high-start approach for a longer canyon-and-ridge route.
permitsClimbing: No permit is required for day hiking or dispersed overnight camping in the Alta Toquima Wilderness. Backcountry camping is free. Visitors should confirm current Humboldt-Toiyabe National Forest rules, seasonal fire restrictions, and road conditions before departure.
permitsCamping: Within Alta Toquima Wilderness, motorized equipment, bicycles, mountain bikes, and other wheeled or mechanized transport are prohibited. Established campsites are limited due to the exposed ridge-top nature of the climb. Most parties camp in Meadow Canyon or at lower elevations within the Pine Creek drainage system. Water is available in creek systems at lower elevations; parties should confirm water availability before committing to high-altitude camps. Pine Creek Campground is generally open May 15 through October 31, weather permitting, and is free. Dispersed camping rules apply: camp at least 100–200 feet from water sources, avoid established social trails, and pack all waste. Visitors should confirm any current group-size limits with the managing agency; in any case, smaller parties are better suited to this exposed alpine terrain. Campfires are generally inappropriate at higher elevations due to sparse fuel, fragile alpine vegetation, and seasonal fire restrictions. Cook stoves are recommended.
wildlife: Late May through October is the standard climbing window on the Jefferson Summit approach. No specialized mountaineering skills are required on the South Summit route described here, but route-finding on the ridge system benefits from compass navigation and basic topographic map reading in poor visibility or winter conditions. Summer thunderstorms are common in July and August, developing rapidly in afternoon hours. Wind exposure on the ridge is persistent and can be severe in spring and fall. Winter ascents are feasible but uncommon; snow and ice may persist into early summer depending on the prior winter's snowfall. The peak is remote, roughly 50 miles from services in Tonopah.
footnote: South Summit 38.75198°N, 116.92673°W. Jefferson Summit access via Forest Road 44010 (Round Mountain–Meadow Creek Road, ~8,786 ft). Elevation 11,949 ft; prominence 5,876 ft. Alta Toquima Wilderness, Humboldt-Toiyabe National Forest.

overview: Mount Ellen ranks No. 29 among the 57 ultra-prominent peaks in the contiguous United States, rising 11,522 feet in the Henry Mountains of remote southern Utah with 5,842 feet of clean prominence. It is the highest summit in the Henry Mountains and Garfield County on BLM-managed public land reached by dirt roads south of Hanksville. Most hikers use the Bull Creek Pass to Mount Ellen North Ridge route for 3.8 miles round-trip with 1,250 feet of gain on Class 1 terrain.

The Henry Mountains are exceptionally remote, with minimal infrastructure, limited development, and dirt-road access that shapes every Mount Ellen attempt. The peak stands directly adjacent to Mount Ellen Peak, 11,506 feet and just 16 feet lower, making it possible to traverse between both summits in a single outing. The range is often described as the last major mountain range in the contiguous United States to be formally mapped by Euro-American survey parties, with Powell's expedition reaching the area in the 1870s. A free-ranging bison herd inhabits the Henry Mountains region.
routeDescription: The standard route follows a worn hiking route from Bull Creek Pass trailhead (10,500 ft), ascending the north ridge through rolling alpine terrain. The route climbs approximately 1,250 feet over 3.8 miles round-trip in 2–4 hours. The path is generally clear and well-used in normal snow-free conditions, passing through tundra and scattered rock to a false summit near 11,400 feet. The true summit lies beyond and is distinguished by a mailbox registry. These distance and gain figures assume road access to Bull Creek Pass; starting from Lonesome Beaver Campground adds approximately 12.4 miles round-trip and 3,250 feet of elevation gain. An optional traverse to Mount Ellen Peak adds approximately 400 feet of descent and re-ascent with 30–45 minutes of additional Class 2–3 scrambling on steep rocky terrain.

The Henry Mountains are accessed via remote dirt and rock roads from Hanksville, Utah. Bull Creek Pass trailhead is located approximately 1 hour south of Hanksville and requires a vehicle capable of handling dirt/rock road surfaces. Roads may wash out during precipitation or snowmelt. Check with the BLM Hanksville-Henry Mountains Field Office or local recreation guides for current road conditions before departure.
permitsClimbing: No standard climbing permit is generally required for Mount Ellen. The peak is managed by the BLM and receives zero permit fees. The region is typically open to public access without standard hiking registration requirements or usage quotas. However, visitors should confirm current regulations and any temporary land-use notices with the BLM Hanksville-Henry Mountains Field Office before departure.
permitsCamping: Wild camping is typically permitted on surrounding BLM lands. Bull Creek Pass area is the primary staging point when road access is clear. Lonesome Beaver Campground (8,215 ft) is the established alternate used for accessing Mount Ellen when the Bull Creek Pass road is snow-blocked in early season; this approach adds approximately 12.4 miles round-trip and 3,250 feet of elevation gain. The campground typically offers vault toilets and picnic facilities; confirm potable water availability before relying on it. Campfires may be allowed when seasonal fire restrictions are not in effect, but stove use is the safer low-impact option. Water sources are limited on the upper route; carry sufficient capacity from the trailhead. Confirm current fire restrictions and camping policies with the BLM office before your visit.
wildlife: No technical climbing skills are required on the standard route in normal snow-free conditions. The route is a non-technical hike, but parties should be comfortable with high-altitude hiking, exposed weather, and basic route awareness near the false summit. The optional Mount Ellen Peak traverse is more difficult than the standard Mount Ellen route and includes Class 2–3 scrambling. Afternoon thunderstorms are common from July through August, with over 30% of annual precipitation falling during summer months as heavy thunderstorms. The upper ridge is fully exposed with minimal shelter; lightning is a serious hazard. Early-morning alpine starts or afternoon descents are standard practice for avoiding electrical storms. Road access to Bull Creek Pass can be blocked by snow into late June or even early July in heavy snow years. If the road is impassable, hiking is feasible from Lonesome Beaver Campground, adding approximately 12.4 miles round-trip and 3,250 feet of elevation gain. A free-ranging bison herd inhabits the Henry Mountains. Observe from a safe distance; stay at least 100 yards away when possible. Never approach for photographs or closer observation. High-altitude exposure with minimal tree cover creates significant UV hazard; sunscreen is essential.
footnote: Bull Creek Pass trailhead: N 37.9267° W 110.5683°. Elevation 11,522 ft; prominence 5,842 ft. Standard route stats assume Bull Creek Pass road access (3.8 miles round-trip, 1,250 ft gain). Mount Ellen Peak (11,506 ft) optional traverse is Class 2–3 and significantly more difficult than the standard route.

overview: Deseret Peak ranks No. 30 among the 57 ultra-prominent peaks in the contiguous United States, rising 11,031 feet in the Stansbury Range of western Utah with 5,807 feet of clean prominence. It is the highest point in Tooele County within the Deseret Peak Wilderness west of Salt Lake City. Most hikers use the Mill Fork Trail from Loop Campground for 7.8 miles round-trip with 3,613 feet of gain on Class 1 terrain.

The standard route up Deseret Peak is short enough for a day hike but steep enough to demand real effort. The landscape—aspen groves, alpine meadows, and loose rocky slopes—is typical of mountain ranges west of the Wasatch, but shaped by the drier climate of the Great Basin. From the summit, the view extends west across the Bonneville Salt Flats and east into the Wasatch Mountains; on clear days, distant landmarks include Pilot Peak nearly 60 miles northwest in Nevada and Mount Nebo roughly 60 miles southeast. The peak receives far less visitation than many nearby Wasatch summits, making it a quieter ultra-prominent objective west of Salt Lake City.
routeDescription: The Mill Fork Trail is the standard and most direct route to Deseret Peak. The route covers 7.8 miles round-trip, gaining 3,613 feet over 5 to 7 hours of hiking. The trail begins at the Mill Fork Trailhead near Loop Campground at approximately 7,418 feet, climbs through aspen forest for roughly 0.75 miles, and crosses South Willow Creek before reaching a junction. The left fork continues toward Deseret Peak; the right branch leads to Willow Lakes. Past the junction, the trail enters Mill Fork valley and climbs steeply through meadows with increasingly open views of Deseret Peak's southwest face. At the head of the canyon, the trail reaches a saddle and then ascends via switchbacks on loose, rocky terrain before gaining the summit ridge and reaching the peak. The descent follows the same route.
permitsClimbing: No climbing permit is required to ascend Deseret Peak. Campground fees apply for overnight stays at Loop Campground; verify current day-use parking and access fees before travel.
permitsCamping: Developed campsites at Loop Campground near the trailhead offer vault restrooms; potable water may not be available, so campers should bring all drinking water. Upper Narrows and Lower Narrows campgrounds, also managed by the Forest Service, provide additional overnight options accessible from South Willow Canyon Road on a first-come, first-served basis. Overnight camping within Deseret Peak Wilderness is governed by Forest Service wilderness regulations. Current rules cover group-size limits, campsite setbacks from water sources and trails, fire restrictions, and prohibitions on motorized and mechanized equipment. Verify specific requirements with the Salt Lake Ranger District before overnight travel, as regulations may change seasonally.
wildlife: The Mill Fork Trail is classified as YDS Class 1 hiking, but the upper mountain features rough, loose, and steeply graded terrain that slows travel and demands careful footing. Loose scree covers much of the upper slopes and especially affects the descent. Parties consistently report the difficulty of route-finding on the descent due to the presence of multiple false summits; identifying the main peak before heading down is critical to avoid wasting time and effort. There is no reliable water available along the Mill Fork Trail in typical summer conditions. Hikers should carry all drinking water from the trailhead and plan accordingly. Snow patches may persist in the Mill Fork drainage into early summer, often around mid-June. Afternoon thunderstorms and weather changes are common at this elevation; summit conditions can differ significantly from weather in the surrounding desert basins.
footnote: Summit coordinates approximately 40.45948°N, 112.62630°W. Mill Fork Trailhead near Loop Campground approximately 40.48431°N, 112.60589°W. Elevation 11,031 ft; prominence 5,807 ft. Verify coordinates against current map, GPS track, or Forest Service source before travel.

overview: Mount Jefferson (OR) ranks No. 31 among the 57 ultra-prominent peaks in the contiguous United States, rising 10,497 feet in the Cascade Range of central Oregon with 5,770 feet of clean prominence. It is Oregon's second-highest summit, ending in a steep Class 4 summit pinnacle within the Mount Jefferson Wilderness. Most climbers use the South Ridge via Pamelia Lake for roughly 13 to 17 miles round-trip with about 6,400 to 8,100 feet of gain on Class 4 snow and rock. Central Cascades Wilderness permits are required June 15 through October 15 via Recreation.gov.

Mount Jefferson is a deeply eroded stratovolcano with no known Holocene eruptions; USGS places its most recent eruptive activity during the last major glaciation, culminating approximately 15,000 years ago. Its flanks hold numerous snow and ice features, including four glaciers consistently named on USGS maps: Whitewater, Jefferson Park, Russell, and Waldo. The surrounding Mount Jefferson Wilderness encompasses approximately 111,000 acres and more than 150 lakes, with Pamelia Lake serving as a common access point for the standard southwest-side climbing route. The eastern side of the mountain lies near the Warm Springs Indian Reservation boundary; public climbing access generally uses west- and north-side national forest approaches.
routeDescription: The standard approach begins at Pamelia Lake Trailhead (3,100 ft) and follows the trail to Pamelia Lake (4,900 ft), then continues toward Hunts Cove and higher camps below the South Ridge. Shale Lake (5,800 ft) is a common overnight camp for South Ridge parties, with established durable sites in the area and reliable seasonal water. From Shale Lake, a climbers' trail gains the South Ridge through loose volcanic sand and intermittent snowfields before reaching Red Saddle (approximately 9,900 ft), the key notch immediately below the summit block.

The final 400–600 feet vary sharply with conditions. In snow-covered conditions, parties often make an exposed snowfield traverse with severe runout below, potentially involving a bergschrund, rimaye, or moat crossing. When the snow has melted back, the route may shift onto direct scrambling on loose volcanic rock around the summit pinnacle. Both options require confident movement on exposed terrain, plus sound judgment about whether to rope up. The summit block is commonly described as Class 4 when snow and ice link the upper route, with low-5th loose volcanic rock possible in late-season bare conditions.

Alternative approaches exist via Jefferson Park from the north (Whitewater Trailhead) and via the Milk Creek drainage on the northwest, both involving additional mileage with similar technical demands above the snowline.
permitsClimbing: The Pamelia Lake Trailhead is managed under the Central Cascades Wilderness Permit system. From June 15 through October 15, day-use permits are required for entry at this trailhead ($1 per person), and overnight permits are required for all overnight trips in the Mount Jefferson Wilderness ($6 per group reservation fee). Permits are reserved through Recreation.gov; fees and release windows are subject to change and should be verified before departure at fs.usda.gov or Recreation.gov. A federal recreation pass (America the Beautiful or equivalent) is also required at the Pamelia Lake Trailhead. Demand at Pamelia Lake can fill on peak summer weekends; advance booking is advisable for July entries.
permitsCamping: Shale Lake is commonly used by South Ridge parties; Jefferson Park Scout Lake (5,900 ft) serves as an alternative for parties approaching from the north. Camp at least 100 feet from water sources. Campfires are not allowed above 5,700 feet and may be subject to additional seasonal restrictions; use a backpacking stove. Store food securely in a bear-resistant canister or by a proper food-hanging method where allowed; pack out all food scraps and trash. Group size is limited to 12 in designated areas.
wildlife: Late June to mid-July is generally the preferred climbing window when snowfields are consolidated and firm. Late-season bare-rock conditions increase technical difficulty and rockfall exposure. Black bears inhabit the Mount Jefferson Wilderness; use bear-resistant food storage and keep a clean camp.
footnote: Trailhead: Pamelia Lake Trailhead, accessed from Highway 22 via Pamelia Creek Road (Forest Road 2246), approximately 12.5 miles east of Detroit, Oregon. Verify current road conditions with the Willamette National Forest before departure.

overview: Pilot Peak ranks No. 33 among the 57 ultra-prominent peaks in the contiguous United States, rising 10,719 feet in the Pilot Range of northeastern Nevada near the Utah border with 5,725 feet of clean prominence. It dominates the high desert between the Bonneville Salt Flats and the Great Basin interior on BLM-managed land. Most hikers and scramblers use the Miners Canyon Route for 4.4 miles round-trip with 2,400 feet of gain on Class 3 terrain.

John C. Fremont named the peak during his third expedition across the Great Basin in the 1840s, recognizing its pyramidal profile as a navigational landmark visible from great distances. The summit retains stone wind shelters and rock alignments whose origins have sparked historical interest. Some accounts attribute them to Civil War-era signaling use, though the best-documented occupation was by William Eimbeck of the U.S. Coast and Geodetic Survey, who established a heliograph station on the summit in July 1889 as part of the 39th parallel survey. The remote location and steep terrain keep visitation low despite the peak's prominence and location on Bureau of Land Management land.
routeDescription: The Miners Canyon route is the standard approach for most parties attempting the summit. From the Miners Canyon Trailhead at approximately 8,300 feet, the route gains roughly 2,400 feet in about 2.2 miles through steep gullies, sagebrush, and increasingly loose talus fields. The terrain steepens significantly as the route gains elevation; many trip reports describe the climb as relentless loose volcanic rock and boulder fields with minimal respite. After reaching the ridge crest at approximately 9,300 feet, the route follows the exposed ridge crest northward with Class 2–3 scrambling across unstable talus terrain and significant exposure before the final summit approach. The descent is typically slower than the ascent due to the loose footing and route-finding challenges on steep terrain. Round-trip hiking time typically ranges from 7 to 10 hours depending on fitness and route-finding speed, though experienced parties report the ascent is considerably more difficult than the mileage and elevation gain might initially suggest.
permitsClimbing: Pilot Peak sits on public land administered by the Bureau of Land Management with no climbing permits or fees required. The peak is managed by the Nevada BLM Elko District Office, and public access is unrestricted. Be aware that while Pilot Mountain Road is public, ranch roads and certain private parcels border the approach area. Confirm access routing through current Nevada BLM resources or local contacts before starting the approach.
permitsCamping: Dispersed camping is available on surrounding BLM lands at no cost, but the remote location demands complete self-sufficiency. There are no developed campgrounds, running water, or facilities on or near the standard route. Water sources in the area are extremely limited; parties typically carry all water for the climb or pre-position supplies near the trailhead. There are no established campsites on the route itself, and the terrain is not conducive to comfortable high camps. Most parties complete the climb in a single long day from the trailhead.
wildlife: The route involves Class 2–3 scrambling with significant exposure on ridge terrain, comfortable route-finding on unmarked, untracked terrain without a clear path, and efficient movement across steep loose rock and boulder fields. Loose volcanic rock and unstable talus are present throughout the route; poor rock quality makes footing unreliable. Significant exposure on steep ridge terrain carries serious fall potential on loose footing. Afternoon wind on the exposed ridge can bring sudden weather deterioration. Dehydration risk is high due to minimal water availability and intense desert sun exposure. Cellular service is minimal; self-rescue is essential due to the remote location far from emergency services.
footnote: Summit 41.0210959°N, 114.0772837°W. Miners Canyon Trailhead N 41.0156° W 114.0744° (~8,300 ft). Elevation 10,719 ft; prominence 5,725 ft. Nevada BLM Elko District.

overview: Crazy Peak ranks No. 32 among the 57 ultra-prominent peaks in the contiguous United States, rising 11,214 feet in the Crazy Mountains of south-central Montana with 5,733 feet of clean prominence. It is the highest summit in the isolated Crazy Mountains north of Big Timber, where public and private lands are intermingled around common approaches. Most hikers and scramblers use the West Ridge Route for 12.7 miles round-trip with 4,809 feet of gain on Class 3 terrain with sustained exposure. Confirm legal access before departure.

The Crazy Mountains hold cultural significance as a vision quest site for the Crow Nation; regional accounts describe Chief Plenty Coups receiving an important guiding vision in the range. The West Ridge ascent combines moderate scrambling, sustained exposure, and broad views across the prairie and toward the Beartooth, Absaroka, and Bridger ranges. Mountain goats frequent the ridge sections, and a glacier persists on the northeast slope year-round.
routeDescription: The standard West Ridge route is approximately 12.7 miles round-trip from Half Moon Campground, gaining 4,809 feet over 10 to 14 hours. The Big Timber Creek Trail ascends a well-maintained path with multiple creek crossings to Blue Lake at approximately 7,000 feet, then continues to the base of the West Ridge. From the saddle near 8,900 feet, the route enters loose scree and Class 2-3 scrambling on the West Ridge proper. The crux occurs in the final approach: a 20-foot descent down a crack passage, a traverse around two rock pinnacles (the most technical section), and a final 30-foot scramble to the true summit. Route-finding is generally straightforward in clear conditions, with cairns often marking the way, but parties should still carry a map, compass/GPS, and be prepared to navigate independently.

Established alternatives include the North Ridge (Class 3, more exposed and less frequently climbed) and the Crazy Couloir (45-degree terrain, serious snow and ice hazard). Twin Lakes offers another approach variation from the southwest.
permitsClimbing: No climbing permit or registration is required for Crazy Peak. The peak falls within the Big Timber Ranger District of the Custer Gallatin National Forest.
permitsCamping: Dispersed/backcountry camping is generally allowed on National Forest land in the Crazy Mountains, subject to current Forest Service regulations, fire restrictions, food-storage rules, and private-land boundaries. Half Moon Campground, the primary trailhead, offers established camping for a fee; check current Forest Service information for seasonal status, rates, and vehicle charges. Water is available from Big Timber Creek along the approach. Because public and private lands are intermingled around the Crazy Mountains, confirm legal access before departure and avoid crossing private land without permission. Bear canisters are recommended for food storage, as grizzly bear activity is possible in this region; bear boxes are not provided at Half Moon Campground. Check current Custer Gallatin National Forest regulations for group size, camping, fire restrictions, and food-storage requirements before departure.
wildlife: The West Ridge route is Class 2-3 scrambling with sections of sustained exposure. Exposure on the north side reaches 1,000+ feet with manageable drop-offs for experienced scramblers. Loose talus and scree are common throughout the upper sections. The final approach to the true summit is the most technical section, requiring careful foot placement and route awareness. Weather deteriorates rapidly in the Crazy Mountains. Afternoon thunderstorms are common from July through August, and extreme wind exposure is typical on ridge sections above 10,000 feet. Snow is possible into June and early July. Strong winds can develop quickly, especially in late afternoon. Rockfall is a meaningful hazard on the upper route, especially on loose scree and talus. Helmets are recommended when multiple parties are on the mountain or when climbing below other groups. The climbing season runs July through September, with the peak season from mid-July through mid-August. June ascents are possible with snow travel skills and microspikes or light crampons for consolidated snow. Late August through September offers more stable conditions with less snow and fewer afternoon thunderstorms than July and August.
footnote: Summit coordinates 46.01815°N, 110.27667°W (ListsOfJohn). Elevation 11,214 ft; prominence 5,733 ft (Montana #1 by prominence). Trailhead: Half Moon Campground (~4,900 ft). Custer Gallatin National Forest, Big Timber Ranger District.

overview: McDonald Peak ranks No. 34 among the 57 ultra-prominent peaks in the contiguous United States, rising 9,820 feet in the Mission Mountains of northwestern Montana with 5,636 feet of clean prominence. It is the highest summit in the Mission Mountains on Flathead Indian Reservation tribal wilderness land. Access is shaped by a mandatory Flathead Reservation Conservation License and a seasonal grizzly bear closure from July 15 through October 1. Most parties approach via Red Horn Lane toward Duncan Lake for 10 to 15 miles round-trip with more than 4,000 feet of gain on Class 3 terrain.

The peak sits within a tightly regulated tribal wilderness area managed by the Confederated Salish and Kootenai Tribes. Access is restricted by a mandatory seasonal closure beginning July 15 for grizzly bear conservation. The closure protects grizzly bears that gather at high elevation to feed on army cutworm moths, which concentrate in alpine talus fields during July, August, and September. This closure compresses the practical climbing window into a short early-season period, generally from early June through July 14, depending on snowpack, road access, and current tribal regulations.
routeDescription: The standard approach begins from a high ridge trailhead accessed via the Red Horn Lane / Ashley Lakes side, requiring high-clearance 4WD vehicles and experienced drivers on rough terrain. From the trailhead, the route gains approximately 4,000+ feet over 10–15 miles round trip, requiring 8–14 hours or an overnight expedition. Parties ascend toward Duncan Lake, traverse the high country toward Post Creek Saddle near 8,900 feet, then traverse the final alpine ridge to the summit. The traverse crosses complex terrain with sustained elevation loss and gain, demanding careful route-finding and solid judgment in early-season snow. Class 2–3 scrambling on the upper slopes requires comfort with exposure and loose ground. An ice axe and crampons are often essential in June and may remain necessary into July depending on snow conditions. The route is remote and committing, well beyond easy self-rescue reach.

Western approaches from the Glacier Lake side may provide alternative vehicle access but involve different route logistics and remain highly committing; experienced parties report day-hike feasibility only under excellent conditions.
permitsClimbing: Climbing on the Flathead Indian Reservation requires a Flathead Reservation Conservation License for non-members. Overnight users also require a Flathead Reservation Camping License ($100). Non-member Conservation Licenses cost $100 for an annual license, $80 for a three-day permit, or $105 for a combined three-day fishing and conservation license. Licenses can be obtained through the Flathead Tribes' Recreation Department or authorized vendors throughout western Montana. Contact the Tribal Recreation Office in Pablo at (406) 675-2700 to confirm current regulations, availability, and road access conditions.
permitsCamping: Commonly used backcountry camping areas have been reported around Duncan Lake and in the Post Creek basin, but overnight use requires the appropriate tribal authorization. Bear country rules are strict on tribal lands. All food, garbage, cooking gear, containers, pet items, and other attractants must be stored in a bear-resistant manner when unattended—either in an approved bear-resistant container or suspended at least 10 feet up and 4 feet out from an upright support. Campfire regulations vary with season and location; verify current fire rules before travel. Tribal wilderness rules prohibit cutting live vegetation and restrict fuel gathering near water sources, trails, and campsites. Groups in the Mission Mountains Tribal Wilderness may not exceed eight people and eight head of livestock unless approved in writing by the Tribes. Camp at least 100 feet from all water sources.
wildlife: Early June through July 14 is the practical climbing window before the McDonald Peak Grizzly Bear Closure Area takes effect. Early-season snow and ice at higher elevations often require self-arrest skills with an ice axe and crampons. McDonald Glacier near 8,600 feet presents cornice, moat, and crevasse hazards on north-facing terrain. Exposed Class 2–3 scrambling with complex route-finding dominates the upper route. Remote terrain beyond easy self-rescue reach offers limited cell service. Bear spray and strict bear-resistant food storage are strongly recommended on tribal lands.
footnote: Summit 47.38269°N, 113.91945°W. Red Horn Lane trailhead N 47.37372° W 113.99283° (~4,987 ft). Elevation 9,820 ft; prominence 5,636 ft. Flathead Indian Reservation, Confederated Salish & Kootenai Tribes.

overview: South Sister ranks No. 35 among the 57 ultra-prominent peaks in the contiguous United States, rising 10,363 feet in the Cascade Range of central Oregon with 5,590 feet of clean prominence. It is the highest of the Three Sisters volcanoes and Oregon's third-highest summit within the Three Sisters Wilderness. Most hikers use the South Sister Climber Trail from Devil's Lake for 13 miles round-trip with 4,950 feet of gain on Class 2 terrain. Central Cascades Wilderness permits are required June 15 through October 15 via Recreation.gov.

Geologically, South Sister is the youngest of the Three Sisters volcanoes; its most recent eruption occurred about 2,000 years ago, and the USGS has documented earthquake swarms and ground uplift southwest of the summit in recent decades. The mountain supports several glaciers, including Prouty Glacier, widely cited as Oregon's largest glacier. At the summit crater sits Teardrop Pool, commonly cited as the highest lake in Oregon. Unlike North Sister and many snowy-season routes on Middle Sister, South Sister's standard summer route is nontechnical and accessible to fit hikers in good conditions, which accounts for its status as a high-demand day-use permit in the Central Cascades Wilderness system.
routeDescription: The standard route departs from Devil's Lake Trailhead at approximately 5,480 feet. Trail access from the trailhead area now uses the Elk-Devils Trail highway underpass rather than a direct highway crossing; check current trailhead instructions on Recreation.gov before departure. From the trailhead, the route climbs through conifer forest for roughly two miles, gaining about 1,400 feet before the trees open onto a broad sandy volcanic plateau. Broken Top and the full south face of South Sister come into view here.

From the plateau, the trail continues northeast across open terrain past Moraine Lake junction (approximately mile 3.2, 7,400 ft) before pitching steeply toward the Lewis Glacier terminal moraine at roughly 8,900 feet. The upper mountain is direct and unrelenting, with few meaningful switchbacks and a sustained grade on loose volcanic scree that gains roughly 1,500 feet to the crater rim at approximately 10,200 feet. A short traverse right along the crater rim reaches the summit. Most parties complete the 12–13 mile round trip in 8–10 hours.

Alternative access routes include the Green Lakes Trailhead to the north, which adds mileage but draws from a separate permit quota, and Todd Lake Trailhead, also an option when Devil's Lake permits are sold out.
routeLandmarks: Lewis Glacier terminal moraine (~8,900 ft, ~4.7 mi)
Summit crater and true summit high point (10,363 ft)
permitsClimbing: South Sister falls within the Central Cascades Wilderness Permit area, administered by the Deschutes National Forest. Permits are required from June 15 through October 15. Day-use permits are issued per person at $1 per person per entry; overnight permits cover groups of up to 12 at $6 per group. Permits are not available at the trailhead or at Forest Service offices; they must be reserved through Recreation.gov. Day-use permits are released in 10-day and 2-day rolling windows at 7:00 AM Pacific time daily. Overnight permit release dates and inventory distribution vary by year; Recreation.gov posts the current season's release schedule before the permit window opens. Weekday dates and shoulder-season windows in late June and early October are usually less competitive than peak summer weekends. A Northwest Forest Pass or America the Beautiful Pass is required for trailhead parking. Permit quotas, fees, and release windows can change; confirm details on Recreation.gov before planning a trip.
permitsCamping: Overnight permit holders are generally not assigned designated campsites, but must follow all wilderness regulations, closure areas, group-size limits, and required setbacks from lakes, streams, and trails. Moraine Lake (approximately 7,400 ft) is the primary on-route overnight area and is managed as a designated camping basin; overnight campers must use posted sites and leave the basin if all designated sites are full. Green Lakes Basin, a viable alternative for parties approaching from the north, operates under the same designated-site rules. Campfires are prohibited above 5,700 feet; use a stove for all camps above treeline. The summit crater rim has informal rock windbreaks, but overnight camping there is exposed, environmentally fragile, and not recommended. Maximum overnight stay is 14 consecutive days. Groups of up to 12 are permitted on an overnight permit.
wildlife: July through September is the standard climbing season. May through June ascents may require crampons and ice axe above 8,000 feet. Afternoon convective storms are common in July and August; plan an early start and aim to clear the exposed upper mountain before early afternoon. The standard route does not require glacier travel; avoid entering glacier terrain without proper equipment and training.
footnote: Trailhead: Devil's Lake Trailhead (N 43.9793°, W 121.7415°), Deschutes National Forest. Access via Cascade Lakes Highway, which is seasonally closed in winter and spring; confirm road status before shoulder-season trips.

overview: Sierra Blanca Peak ranks No. 36 among the 57 ultra-prominent peaks in the contiguous United States, rising 11,975 feet in the Sierra Blanca Range of southern New Mexico with 5,543 feet of clean prominence. It is the highest summit in the southern half of New Mexico on sacred Mescalero Apache Reservation land. Most hikers use the Lookout Mountain Ridge Route for 9 to 12 miles round-trip with 3,000 to 3,500 feet of gain on Class 2 terrain. Current summit access must be verified directly with the Mescalero Apache Tribe because permit availability and public climbing permission are uncertain.

Sierra Blanca is part of an ancient volcanic complex whose high ridges preserve evidence of volcanic and glacial history. Weather patterns are unpredictable at elevation; thunderstorms develop rapidly in the summer monsoon season, and snow can linger into June or return as early as October. Despite the moderate Class 1–2 technical rating of most routes, the exposed ridge approach, thin air at nearly 12,000 feet, and fire-damaged terrain on upper slopes create objective hazards beyond basic hiking.
routeDescription: The most commonly used approach to Sierra Blanca gains 3,000 to 3,500 feet over 9 to 12 miles round-trip, depending on trailhead and access point. The route ascends toward Lookout Mountain on the ridge system, passing through mixed conifer forest before breaking into alpine terrain. Beyond Lookout Mountain, the trail becomes faint and the route transitions to low Class 2 ridge walking and light scrambling on exposed high-country terrain. Navigation is essential on the final approach; parties should expect loose talus, rockfall hazard from fire-scarred slopes, and wind-swept ridge conditions.

Longer approaches through the White Mountain Wilderness trail system exist, but they do not necessarily provide legal access to the summit if the final route crosses tribal land. Ski Apache operates seasonally with resort facilities including gondola and hiking access, but resort amenities do not grant summit permission. Verify boundaries and current tribal access authorization before planning any approach.
routeLandmarks: Ice Spring area (historic map reference; water reliability uncertain)
permitsClimbing: Sierra Blanca Peak's summit lies on Mescalero Apache Reservation land. The Mescalero Apache Tribe officially requires permits for summit access and has issued statements indicating that climbing may no longer be allowed due to the sacred status of the mountain. Permit availability and current access policies are uncertain. Contact the Mescalero Apache Travel Office at (575) 464-4494 to determine current access status before planning an ascent. Enforcement of access restrictions varies by season and conditions (fire bans, drought restrictions, etc.). Do not assume that prior trip reports, Ski Apache resort facilities, or gondola rides authorize summit access beyond the resort boundary.
permitsCamping: Backcountry camping may be available on portions of Lincoln National Forest land, subject to current forest orders and fire restrictions. Overnight camping should be planned only on lands where camping is currently permitted; verify that your intended campsite is not on tribal land, resort property, or in areas with seasonal closures. Tribal, resort, and national forest lands are intermingled in this area, so boundary verification is essential. Water is limited and unreliable on ridge routes; carry sufficient capacity for overnight travel. Confirm current camping regulations and land-use status with Lincoln National Forest before your trip.
wildlife: June through October is the standard climbing window on Sierra Blanca. Afternoon thunderstorms are common at high altitude during the summer monsoon season. Rockfall from fire-scarred slopes with loose rock is a significant hazard on upper sections. Beyond Lookout Mountain, the ridge route becomes faint with low Class 2 scrambling on exposed terrain where navigation is essential. Snow can linger into June or return as early as October; microspikes may be useful during shoulder-season travel.
footnote: Summit 33.37433°N, 105.80871°W. Verify current parking and access points with Ski Apache or Lincoln National Forest. Elevation 11,975 ft; prominence 5,543 ft. Mescalero Apache Reservation / Lincoln National Forest.

overview: Pikes Peak ranks No. 37 among the 57 ultra-prominent peaks in the contiguous United States, rising 14,115 feet in the Front Range above Colorado Springs, Colorado with 5,530 feet of clean prominence. It is the easternmost ultra-prominent peak in the contiguous United States. Most hikers use the Barr Trail for roughly 23 to 29 miles round-trip with about 6,550 to 8,350 feet of gain on Class 1 terrain. Barr Trailhead parking fees apply in peak season.

Pikes Peak's cultural history includes its association with "America the Beautiful" and with Zebulon Pike's 1806 expedition. The mountain's visibility from the plains, summit road, cog railway, and long Barr Trail approach make it unusual among Colorado fourteeners: it is both highly developed and still a major hiking objective from Manitou Springs.
routeDescription: The Barr Trail is the standard hiking route to the summit. The route is 13 miles one-way with 7,450 feet of elevation gain. The ascent is relentless and well-defined, with the initial section featuring steady switchbacks through the "Ws" as the trail climbs Mount Manitou.
