No-Name Creek appears at roughly 5.5 miles and marks the transition from Mount Manitou to the base of Pikes Peak proper, often providing seasonal water. Most hikers cover the first 6.5 miles to Barr Camp in 2 to 4 hours, depending on fitness and acclimatization.

Barr Camp sits at 10,200 feet and is the main halfway landmark on the route, with overnight lodging by reservation and nearby filterable water. From Barr Camp, the route continues roughly 6.5 more miles to the summit, gaining nearly 4,000 additional feet. The trail remains defined but becomes increasingly exposed above treeline. The final high-elevation section passes through rocky, uneven terrain near the 16 Golden Stairs before reaching the summit area.

Because the summit is accessible by road and cog railway, some hikers arrange a ride down rather than descending the full Barr Trail; confirm transportation plans in advance rather than assuming space or availability.
routeLandmarks: No-Name Creek (~5.5 miles): Seasonal water source before treeline
Barr Camp (~6.5 miles, ~10,200 ft): Overnight lodge, day-use facility, and nearby filterable water
A-Frame Shelter (~12,098 ft): Unstaffed shelter below the upper mountain
16 Golden Stairs (~13,500+ ft): Final rocky section
permitsClimbing: No climbing permit is required for the standard Barr Trail ascent. Parking at the Barr Trailhead in Manitou Springs requires a fee; current rates are typically $25–$32.50 per day. Parking is limited and should be arranged in advance during peak season. Road, parking, and summit access rules can change; verify current conditions before traveling.
permitsCamping: Barr Camp, located at roughly 6.5 miles and 10,200 feet, is the main overnight stop on the Barr Trail. It operates by reservation and offers bunkhouse lodging, lean-tos, and day-use space; amenities vary by reservation type. Water must be filtered or treated from nearby sources. Dispersed camping is allowed on nearby Pike National Forest land where not otherwise posted. Camp at least 100 feet from water sources and trails, follow current fire restrictions, store food securely, and pack out all trash. Camping is limited to 14 days within any continuous 30-day period.
wildlife: Peak hiking season usually runs from June through September, depending on snowpack and storm patterns. July through early September generally offer the most reliable dry-trail conditions but also bring the highest thunderstorm risk. September can bring cooler temperatures and fewer hikers, though early snow becomes more likely. Winter and shoulder-season ascents require traction, winter layers, navigation skills, and weather judgment depending on conditions.
footnote: Standard trailhead: Barr Trailhead, Manitou Springs. Parking reservations and fees apply during peak season; verify current trailhead access and summit transportation options before traveling.

overview: Mount Nebo ranks No. 38 among the 57 ultra-prominent peaks in the contiguous United States, rising 11,928 feet in the Wasatch Range of central Utah, with 5,500 feet of clean prominence. It is the highest and southernmost summit in the Wasatch Range and the high point of Utah County. Most hikers use the North Peak Trail from the North Mount Nebo Trailhead for 9 miles round-trip with about 3,150 feet of gain to the summit on Class 2 terrain.

The peak's name comes from the biblical mountain from which Moses viewed the Promised Land, and the surrounding Mount Nebo Wilderness preserves roughly 28,000 acres of roadless terrain in the southern Wasatch. Nebo's upper flanks are capped in dark limestone and loose shale, the same rock that defines the scrambling crux on the standard route. The roughly 35–38-mile Nebo Loop National Scenic Byway circles the base and is well-known for autumn foliage.

Although visible from much of the Wasatch Front, Nebo sits far enough south of Salt Lake City to see lighter traffic than Timpanogos on most summer weekends. Three distinct summits run north to south along its main ridge; the northernmost is the high point.
routeDescription: The standard approach to the high summit follows North Peak Trail from the North Mount Nebo Trailhead on the east side of the mountain at 9,262 feet. The route covers 9 miles round-trip with 3,150 feet of gain to the summit and approximately 3,650 feet cumulative round-trip. A maintained trail runs the full length of the route, following a fence line from the trailhead before climbing through conifer stands and open ridge terrain. The trail steepens considerably on the approach to Wolf Pass, then ascends the final ridge to the summit on a steep, rocky path with some loose rock and minor exposure. The summit ridge includes multiple high points; the northern summit of Mount Nebo is the high point and the standard objective. Most parties complete the round-trip in 4 to 6 hours.
Other established routes include the Andrews Ridge approach from the south and the Willow Creek Canyon approach from the west, both less frequently used and longer to the high summit.
permitsClimbing: No permit is required to climb Mount Nebo. The North Mount Nebo Trailhead sits on Uinta-Wasatch-Cache National Forest land within the Mount Nebo Wilderness. There is no fee, self-registration, or quota for day use or overnight travel.
permitsCamping: Dispersed camping is permitted throughout the Mount Nebo Wilderness with no designated site requirement. No bear canister mandate is generally associated with this area, but food should be secured from wildlife and never left unattended. Campfires are subject to seasonal restrictions that vary by year; consult the Uinta-Wasatch-Cache National Forest for current closure orders before any overnight trip. Developed campgrounds with vault toilets are available along the Nebo Loop Byway for parties preferring frontcountry options. Water may be limited or absent along the standard route; carry what you need from the trailhead and treat any backcountry water.
wildlife: No specialized technical skills are required for the standard summer route, though loose-rock scrambling experience is helpful for the final ridge pitch. Loose shale and limestone on the upper ridge create rockfall risk in groups. Afternoon thunderstorms are common July through August; the exposed summit ridge offers no shelter. Snow can linger on the upper trail into late June or early July; microspikes are advisable for early-season travel.
footnote: Summit N 39.81610° W 111.65580°. North Mount Nebo Trailhead: N 39.84848° W 111.72217° (9,262 ft). Elevation 11,928 ft; prominence 5,500 ft. Mount Nebo Wilderness, Uinta-Wasatch-Cache National Forest.

overview: Snowshoe Peak ranks No. 39 among the 57 ultra-prominent peaks in the contiguous United States, rising 8,738 feet in the Cabinet Mountains of northwestern Montana with 5,442 feet of clean prominence. It is the highest summit in the Cabinet Range within remote Kootenai National Forest country northeast of Libby. Most climbers use the Northeast Ridge via Leigh Lake for 5 to 7 miles round-trip with 3,600 to 4,200 feet of gain on Class 4 terrain.

The peak's significance lies in its tectonic geography: the Cabinet Mountains form part of the Coeur d'Alene metamorphic core complex, and Snowshoe Peak's prominence reflects its dramatic rise from the surrounding valleys. Blackwell Glacier, a remnant named glacier on the peak's north slope, is an important feature of the mountain, though standard summer ascents of the northeast ridge generally treat the route as exposed scrambling rather than glacier travel.
routeDescription: The standard route approaches from Leigh Lake to the northeast, ascending via exposed scrambling and Class 4 terrain along the peak's northeast ridge. From the Leigh Lake trailhead (accessed via a rocky road requiring high-clearance vehicles), hikers travel approximately 1.5 miles to Leigh Lake at around 5,100 feet, then continue around the northeast side of the lake before ascending a creek drainage to the summit route. From Leigh Lake, the route ascends roughly 3,600 feet of mixed terrain—much of it straightforward hiking and Class 3 scrambling on ledges and chimneys—before reaching the crux: a short but stiff Class 4 chimney with moderate exposure, where solid handholds and ledges provide reasonable climbing features. Above the crux, terrain reverts to scrambling and hiking until reaching the upper ridge sections near the summit.

Blackwell Glacier lies on the peak's north side. Early-season parties and some route variations may encounter glacier or steep-snow sections requiring snow travel skills and crevasse awareness, but standard late-summer ascents on the northeast ridge may navigate around it entirely through scrambling. The upper ridge features significant exposure and loose rock, particularly on descent.
routeLandmarks: Class 4 chimney crux on upper ridge
permitsClimbing: No climbing permit is generally required for Snowshoe Peak. The peak lies within Kootenai National Forest, where dispersed backcountry camping is generally available, subject to current forest and wilderness regulations. Parties should verify current access, camping, and food-storage rules before departure.
permitsCamping: Camping is not allowed at Leigh Lake (per Visit Montana / Forest Service regulations). Parties using the Leigh Lake approach should plan accordingly. On-route camping for the standard northeast ridge approach from Leigh Lake is limited. Snowshoe Lake, on the peak's western side, provides camping around 6,000 feet for parties using or exploring western approaches via longer routes. It is not part of the standard Leigh Lake northeast approach. The Cabinet Mountains Wilderness area applies standard backcountry protocols: camp at least 100 feet from water sources. Campfire rules vary by location and season; check current restrictions with Kootenai National Forest. A stove is the safer and lower-impact choice.
wildlife: July through September is the standard climbing window. Solid Class 4 scrambling ability is required for the crux chimney; rock climbing experience and exposure management on the upper ridge are essential. Self-arrest with an ice axe on moderate snow slopes may be needed seasonally. Rope, harness, and anchor skills are commonly brought for the Class 4 crux and descent. Self-arrest, snow climbing, glacier travel, and crevasse recognition may be needed in early season or on alternate route lines. The Cabinet-Yaak ecosystem supports approximately 20–30 grizzly bears; bear-resistant food storage is required under Kootenai National Forest food-storage and sanitation order.
footnote: Trailhead: Leigh Lake (rocky road, high-clearance vehicle recommended), approximately 17 miles northeast of Libby, MT. Summit 48.2478°N, 115.8958°W. Elevation 8,738 ft (LiDAR/Peakbagger); prominence 5,442 ft (Peakbagger clean prominence). Kootenai National Forest.

overview: North Schell Peak ranks No. 41 among the 57 ultra-prominent peaks in the contiguous United States, rising 11,891 feet in the Schell Creek Range of northeastern Nevada with 5,403 feet of clean prominence. It is the high point of the High Schells Wilderness within Humboldt-Toiyabe National Forest northeast of Ely. Most hikers use the Timber Creek Trail for 6 to 8 miles round-trip with 2,800 to 3,100 feet of gain on Class 2 terrain.

The range climbs from 6,500 feet in adjacent valleys to the high ridgeline where multiple significant peaks dominate the crest. The peak's position at the center of this ridge system makes it ideal for parties interested in extended ridge traverses linking South Schell Peak and Taft Peak into a longer mountain loop.
routeDescription: The Timber Creek Trail is the standard approach to North Schell Peak. Beginning near Timber Creek Campground at approximately 9,000 feet, the route covers roughly 6 to 8 miles round-trip with about 2,800 to 3,100 feet of elevation gain. Most parties should expect a 5 to 6 hour outing. From the campground trailhead, an established trail follows Timber Creek through aspen and meadow terrain. The trail transitions into a heavily overgrown former 4x4 road that becomes increasingly indistinct. Where the old road ends at approximately 10,500 feet, parties leave trail entirely and walk off-trail over grass, talus, and rocky slopes to gain the ridge crest. Once on the ridge, a social trail provides intermittent route guidance across multiple false summits before the final walk to the summit.
permitsClimbing: No permits are required for day hiking within Humboldt-Toiyabe National Forest. Overnight backpackers may generally use dispersed camping in the surrounding national forest, subject to current Humboldt-Toiyabe National Forest and High Schells Wilderness regulations. For current road, trail, and camping conditions, contact the Ely Ranger District at 775-289-3031.
permitsCamping: Timber Creek Campground is a developed Forest Service group campground near the route, with vault toilets and drinking water provided in season. Current Forest Service information indicates reservations are generally required, and Recreation.gov lists campground fees. It is pack-in/pack-out with no trash collection. Verify seasonal water, road access, reservations, and fees before relying on it as a trailhead basecamp. Additional dispersed camping is available in the surrounding national forest, subject to current Humboldt-Toiyabe National Forest and High Schells Wilderness regulations.
wildlife: Established trail from the campground follows Timber Creek and transitions into a heavily overgrown former 4x4 road. From the road's end at approximately 10,500 feet, the route leaves trail entirely for off-trail Class 2 hiking over grass, talus, and rocky slopes to reach the ridge crest. Once on the ridge, a social trail provides intermittent route guidance. Navigation requires comfort with off-trail travel and the ability to use a map, compass, GPS device, or offline map app. No hands-on climbing is required. Great Basin weather deteriorates rapidly, particularly during July and August when afternoon thunderstorms develop frequently. The exposed alpine ridge carries significant lightning risk. Wind exposure is constant at the summit. Large temperature swings between valley and ridgeline are common; layers are essential. Snow patches may persist into July in years with above-average precipitation. June through October is the typical climbing season; optimal dry conditions are usually mid-July through September. Experienced parties sometimes continue south along the ridgeline to South Schell Peak (11,765 ft) and Taft Peak (11,734 ft), extending the outing into a loop hike with additional exposure and unobstructed Great Basin vistas.
footnote: Elevation 11,891 ft (LiDAR); USGS/topographic sources commonly list 11,883 ft. Prominence 5,403 ft. Summit 39.41330°N, 114.59980°W. North Fork Timber Creek Trailhead near Timber Creek Campground 39.40025°N, 114.61907°W. Ely Ranger District: 775-289-3031.

overview: Star Peak ranks No. 42 among the 57 ultra-prominent peaks in the contiguous United States, rising 9,837 feet in the Humboldt Range of north-central Nevada with 5,400 feet of clean prominence. It is the highest summit in the Humboldt Range north of Lovelock. Most hikers use the El Dorado Canyon Road Approach for 6 to 10 miles round-trip with 3,000 to 5,000 feet of gain on Class 2 terrain across BLM-managed high desert.

The peak's rounded, sage-covered slopes lack dramatic topographic relief when viewed from the highway, which contributes to its overlooked status. Mining activity in the region and rough road conditions deter casual visitors, but clear air and open horizons make the summit distinctive for parties seeking high desert vistas without technical climbing.
routeDescription: El Dorado Canyon Road is one of the commonly described western approaches to Star Peak. The route begins at Interstate 80 Exit 138 and follows rough BLM roads northward into the Humboldt Range for 3 to 5 miles, gaining 1,200 to 1,700 feet depending on where a vehicle can be parked. High-clearance four-wheel drive is strongly recommended for the upper sections, though lower portions may be passable in a standard vehicle in good conditions. Abandoned buildings at approximately 5,700 feet mark a major waypoint where the road deteriorates substantially. From there, most parties either continue hiking the rough road or drive higher if conditions and vehicle capability allow. The remaining summit climb from the upper road area is a short off-road hike over open sage slopes, gaining 1,300 to 2,000 additional feet depending on parking location. Total distance ranges from 6 to 10 miles round-trip with 3,000 to 5,000 feet of elevation gain, depending on vehicle capability and parking decisions. The route is Class 2 throughout, mainly due to off-trail sagebrush slopes and uneven rocky road surfaces, with straightforward route-finding on the open upper slopes. Typical parties report 5 to 8 hours round-trip from their parking point, depending on road conditions, parking location, and pace.

Black Canyon and Star City approaches also provide access to the summit, so route details and distances vary significantly by source and current road conditions. A communications antenna near the summit marks the high point.
routeLandmarks: I-80 Exit 138 (El Dorado Canyon approach start)
permitsClimbing: No permits are required to climb Star Peak. The peak lies on Bureau of Land Management land managed by the Winnemucca District Office, and access is generally unrestricted. Climbers should verify current access and road conditions with the Winnemucca District Office (775-623-1500, 5100 East Winnemucca Blvd, Winnemucca, NV 89445), as mining activity and seasonal road damage may affect approach routes.
permitsCamping: Free dispersed camping is available throughout the surrounding BLM land. No developed campgrounds exist in the immediate area, and facilities are minimal in the region. Water sources are scarce on the peak and approach; carrying sufficient water is essential. Dispersed camping regulations require campers to be self-sufficient regarding sanitation and fire management.
wildlife: May through October is the standard climbing window on Star Peak. Competent navigation on rough BLM roads in a high-clearance vehicle and basic route-finding on open sage slopes are the main technical demands. Afternoon thunderstorms are common July through August, with considerable lightning exposure on the open summit. Wind exposure is significant on the upper slopes, and minimal water availability creates dehydration risk in high-desert conditions. Rough road conditions can cause undercarriage damage with inadequate vehicles.
footnote: Summit 40.52244°N, 118.17077°W. El Dorado Canyon approach from I-80 Exit 138. Elevation 9,837 ft; prominence 5,400 ft. Nevada BLM Winnemucca District.

overview: Hayford Peak ranks No. 40 among the 57 ultra-prominent peaks in the contiguous United States, rising 9,917 feet in the Sheep Range of southern Nevada with 5,417 feet of clean prominence. It is the highest summit in the Desert National Wildlife Refuge north of Las Vegas. Most hikers use the Deadman Canyon Route for 15 to 18 miles round-trip with 4,000 to 4,250 feet of gain on Class 2 terrain within the refuge's 1.5 million acres.

The refuge protects remote high-desert terrain in southern Nevada. The Sheep Range ecosystem transitions sharply with elevation: Joshua tree woodland at lower elevations gives way to pinyon-juniper forest, then ponderosa pine, and finally bristlecone pine and sparse subalpine terrain near the summit. Hayford Peak's isolation and remoteness make solitude likely even during peak season, with relatively few party reports annually.
routeDescription: The common Deadman Canyon route begins at Deadman Canyon trailhead (approximately 5,000 feet), accessed from Corn Creek Visitor Center via 15 miles of Alamo Road north, then Hidden Forest Road. The approach gains roughly 1,700 feet over 5 miles through Deadman Canyon to Hidden Forest Cabin at 8,000 feet, ascending through sagebrush slopes and pinyon-juniper woodland. From the cabin, the final several miles present the climbing crux: steep, rocky Class 2 terrain gaining approximately 2,500 feet to the summit ridge. Above 8,000 feet, cairns and use paths guide hikers through bristlecone pine and increasingly open, rocky high-elevation terrain. The upper ridge becomes increasingly exposed and rocky, with the steepest section occupying the final 400 feet. Total distance is 15–18 miles round trip, depending on exact starting point and route variation. Route-finding is straightforward once committed to the ridge, though navigation skills and a topographic map are essential due to the remote location.
permitsClimbing: No backpacking permit is required to climb Hayford Peak. Backcountry camping is free. Visitors should confirm current Desert National Wildlife Refuge rules before traveling, particularly regarding road closures, which change seasonally.
permitsCamping: Backpacking is allowed without a permit. Camps must be established at least 100 yards from springs or water developments to protect water sources and comply with FWS regulations. Campfires are prohibited in the backcountry, including at Hidden Forest Cabin; gas backpacking stoves are allowed. Water is available from nearby springs, though flow varies seasonally and should not be assumed reliable. Wiregrass Spring near Hidden Forest Cabin is the key documented water source for this route, but flow can vary and should not be assumed without recent confirmation. The historic Hidden Forest Cabin sits near 8,000 feet and is a useful landmark and rest point on the route. FWS describes the cabin as a historic structure that is not intended for overnight use, so parties should plan to camp outside while following refuge camping rules. Backpackers commonly use the Hidden Forest area as a base for a second-day summit attempt, but camps must be at least 100 yards from springs or water developments. Dispersed camping is generally allowed in appropriate backcountry areas of the Sheep Range, subject to current refuge restrictions. All camping parties must practice Leave-No-Trace principles.
wildlife: April through October is the standard climbing window on Hayford Peak. The standard route requires no technical climbing skills, but competency with topographic map navigation and basic backcountry route-finding is essential given the remote location and limited trail infrastructure above Hidden Forest Cabin. Rapid elevation gain exposes hikers to sharply changing temperatures, wind, and storm conditions. Summer monsoon storms can build quickly during afternoon hours. Winter and early spring bring significant snowfall at higher elevations; snow and ice conditions can make the upper ridge hazardous even for experienced mountaineers. The rough road access requires advance planning to coordinate vehicle capability with seasonal road conditions. Desert bighorn sheep inhabit the range; mountain lions are possible though rarely encountered.
footnote: Summit 36.6578°N, 115.2008°W. Deadman Canyon / Hidden Forest access from Corn Creek Visitor Center via Alamo Road and Hidden Forest Road. Elevation 9,917 ft; prominence 5,417 ft. Desert National Wildlife Refuge.

overview: Diamond Peak ranks No. 43 among the 57 ultra-prominent peaks in the contiguous United States, rising 12,200 feet in the Lemhi Range of eastern Idaho with 5,377 feet of clean prominence. It is Idaho's fourth-highest summit and the highest peak outside the Lost River Range. Most hikers and scramblers use the East Ridge via Birch Creek Valley for 5 to 6 miles round-trip with 4,000 feet of gain on Class 3 terrain.

The Lemhi Range occupies a unique geomorphic position in the Basin and Range province. The peak's east ridge, offering the standard ascent, presents a steep Idaho ridge scramble concentrated with significant elevation gain in a short distance—approximately 4,000 feet gained in under 3 miles.
routeDescription: The standard route ascends the steep East Ridge, beginning from Birch Creek Campground near the trailhead at approximately 8,200 feet. The route maintains a sustained Class 3 scramble throughout, climbing 4,000 feet in rapid succession with a notable crux in the mid-route section. Most of the elevation gain occurs in the first 3 miles, creating a sustained, nearly 4,000-foot ridge scramble. The final sections lead to a summit plateau with expansive views across eastern Idaho's high country. Staying near the ridge crest provides the most stable terrain; terrain immediately adjacent to the ridge can become Class 4 or steeper, so staying on the ridge line is essential.

The ascent is straightforward in its objective but demanding in its steepness. Loose rock hazards are present throughout, particularly on the mid-route sections where the exposure becomes notable without reaching technical climbing terrain. Wind is common along the ridge, and afternoon thunderstorm potential increases with elevation. An early alpine start is essential—parties should reach the summit well before midday to avoid weather hazard. Parties approaching from the Badger Creek drainage on the west side will encounter a longer, more remote route that rates Class 3 but with less defined travel; this approach is rarely used and requires solid routefinding ability and 4WD access to the trailhead.
permitsClimbing: No climbing permit is generally required. The standard east-side access uses BLM land around Birch Creek and reaches Diamond Peak on Caribou-Targhee National Forest land. Parties should verify current access, camping, fire, and land-management rules before departure.
permitsCamping: Birch Creek Campground is a BLM campground with no standard site fee; group sites require reservations and a fee. The campground frequently fills on summer weekends due to fishing and off-road vehicle traffic in the region. Badger Creek drainage on the west side offers alternative camping at lower demand, with shadier sites and a typically less-crowded atmosphere, though this approach requires 4WD access and is less direct. Camp at least 100 feet from all water sources. Campfire rules vary by season and jurisdiction; use a stove where possible and avoid fires in fragile alpine and subalpine areas.
wildlife: June through September is the standard climbing window. Class 3 scrambling on steep, exposed terrain is required throughout; navigation on the exposed ridge is essential as the route is not marked. Rapid elevation gain—4,000 feet in under 3 miles—requires adequate acclimatization. Afternoon thunderstorms on the fully exposed ridge present a significant lightning hazard.
footnote: Trailhead: Birch Creek Campground, approximately 8,200 feet, accessed from the Birch Creek Valley on the east side of the Lemhi Range, central Idaho. Summit 43.9103°N, 113.0847°W. Elevation 12,200 ft; prominence 5,377 ft (approximate).

overview: Flat Top Mountain ranks No. 44 among the 57 ultra-prominent peaks in the contiguous United States, rising 10,624 feet in the Oquirrh Mountains southwest of Salt Lake City, Utah, with 5,370 feet of clean prominence. It is the highest summit in the Oquirrh Mountains, where Rio Tinto Kennecott mining operations and private landowners control most access. Most hikers use the South Fork Ophir Canyon Route for 8 to 10 miles round-trip with 2,500 to 3,000 feet of gain on Class 1 terrain after obtaining landowner permission.

Access to Flat Top Mountain is heavily constrained by private ownership and nearby Rio Tinto Kennecott mining operations, which control much of the Oquirrh Mountains. Hiking access should not be assumed. Historically, access has sometimes been granted by local landowners on a case-by-case basis. Current permission must be independently verified with the appropriate landowner or land manager before any attempt. Because of these restrictions, Flat Top sees far fewer visitors than comparable prominent peaks near the Salt Lake Valley.
routeDescription: The standard approach follows South Fork Ophir Canyon Road from Ophir (6,700 feet) southeast toward Halls Canyon, where a trail climbs to the ridge junction with Lewiston Peak (10,400 feet). From the ridge, hikers traverse north roughly 0.5 miles to Flat Top's summit (10,624 feet). Total distance is approximately 8–10 miles round-trip with 2,500–3,000 feet of elevation gain. The trail becomes faint or disappears near the upper ridge, where route-finding requires careful attention to multiple false summits and brushy subalpine slopes. Map and GPS are essential to identify the true summit.

The final 3 miles of Ophir Canyon Road are rough and rocky; high-clearance 4WD is recommended for the final miles, especially after storms or during early season. Experienced parties note that whiteout conditions on the featureless plateau can be disorienting, and a topo map, compass, and GPS track are strongly recommended to identify the true summit and avoid drifting onto private or restricted land. The ridge traverse is exposed to afternoon thunderstorm development.
routeLandmarks: Summit ridge junction with Lewiston Peak (~10,400 ft)
permitsClimbing: No wilderness permits are required to climb Flat Top Mountain. Private land access is the primary gate for this peak. Historically, access has sometimes been granted by local landowners on a case-by-case basis. Current permission must be independently verified with the appropriate landowner or land manager before any attempt.
permitsCamping: Camping may be available near the Half Moon area or in the broader Ophir Canyon vicinity where permitted, but land ownership, facilities, water availability, and current restrictions should be verified before planning an overnight trip. Do not assume that camping is allowed along the approach without explicit permission from the appropriate landowner or land manager.
wildlife: Multiple false summits and broad subalpine terrain require careful navigation with map and GPS near the upper ridge and summit plateau. Cross-country travel crosses brushy subalpine slopes, scattered krummholz, and intermittent open terrain; trails become intermittent or disappear entirely. Ridge terrain is exposed to afternoon thunderstorms June through September; wind is constant on the high plateau. Staying on route is essential to avoid trespassing. Current access permission must be independently verified with the appropriate landowner or land manager before any attempt. May may involve lingering snow, mud, and difficult road access; June through October is typically the most reliable hiking season.
footnote: Summit coordinates approximately 40.5864°N, 111.9398°W (verify against USGS GNIS or ListsOfJohn). Ophir town approximately N 40.5894° W 111.9245°. Elevation 10,624 ft; prominence 5,370 ft. Typical time about 6 hours for 9-mile round-trip with ~2,750 ft gain; actual time varies with route-finding, snow, and terrain.

overview: Mount Stuart ranks No. 45 among the 57 ultra-prominent peaks in the contiguous United States, rising 9,415 feet in the Stuart Range of central Washington, with 5,354 feet of clean prominence. It is the highest non-volcanic summit in Washington and the dominant peak of the Alpine Lakes Wilderness. Most climbers use the Cascadian Couloir for 12 to 13 miles round-trip with about 8,000 feet of cumulative gain on Class 3 terrain.

Mount Stuart commands attention not through raw elevation alone but through staggering local relief: the south face rises roughly 5,000 feet in about 2 horizontal miles of granite relief. While the peak is known for excellent rock on many technical routes, the standard Cascadian Couloir follows looser gullies, scree, and broken terrain that define the nontechnical ascent.

The peak was named in September 1853 by surveyor George B. McClellan in honor of a fallen friend. Mount Stuart's proximity to Seattle, combined with its alpine setting, complex route-finding, and surrounding lakes and granite basins, makes it a destination for experienced scramblers and climbers capable of managing loose terrain, rockfall hazard, and extended wilderness approaches.
routeDescription: The Cascadian Couloir is the standard and most frequently traveled nontechnical route to the summit. Parties typically begin at the Esmeralda Trailhead, ascend toward Longs Pass, then descend into the Ingalls Creek basin, a common overnight area. From Ingalls Creek, the route continues toward the south side of Mount Stuart and ascends the Cascadian Couloir, a broad gully system of loose rock, sand, scree, and intermittent boot paths.

The couloir gains roughly 2,000 feet and is notorious for rockfall, especially when multiple parties are above one another. Above the couloir, climbers navigate broken terrain, intermittent cairns, and false summits before reaching the summit area. The easiest line is generally Class 3, with sustained loose Class 2 travel and short sections of exposed scrambling near the top. Strong parties may complete the climb in a long day, but many parties climb it as a two-day trip with a camp near Ingalls Creek.
permitsClimbing: No climbing permit is required for Mount Stuart by the standard Cascadian Couloir route. A valid Northwest Forest Pass or equivalent federal recreation pass is typically required for parking at the Esmeralda Trailhead. Confirm current pass requirements and purchase options with the Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest before departure.
permitsCamping: Ingalls Creek is the common overnight area for parties using the Cascadian Couloir, with established campsites and seasonal water nearby. Above Ingalls Creek, reliable water should not be expected except from seasonal snowmelt. Camp at least 100 feet from water sources and trails, use durable surfaces, and pack out all waste. Other approaches near Ingalls Lake or the Enchantments may be subject to different wilderness rules, seasonal restrictions, or permit zones; confirm current regulations before planning an alternate camp. Campfire restrictions, food-storage requirements, and group-size limits vary by zone and season, so check current Alpine Lakes Wilderness regulations before departure.
wildlife: Mount Stuart demands advanced scrambling ability on steep, loose granite, scree, and talus. Route-finding is difficult on the upper mountain, where confusing paths and intermittent cairns create navigation hazards. Excellent map-reading and navigation skills are essential. Climbers should be comfortable moving on steep, unprotected talus and broken rock, with enough fitness and judgment for self-rescue in remote alpine terrain. Helmet use is strongly advised due to frequent overhead rockfall risk, especially in and below the couloir.
footnote: Sources: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest | USGS | Peakbagger | SummitPost | Cascade Alpine Guides

overview: Blanca Peak ranks No. 46 among the 57 ultra-prominent peaks in the contiguous United States, rising 14,351 feet in the Sangre de Cristo Mountains of southern Colorado with 5,331 feet of clean prominence. It is the fourth-highest peak in Colorado and the highest summit in the Sangre de Cristo Range. Most hikers use the Lake Como Road Route for 14 to 17 miles round-trip with 6,000 to 6,500 feet of gain on Class 2 terrain; private-land boundaries restrict southern and eastern approaches, making Lake Como Road the standard public route.

The Blanca massif sits in the rugged southern Sangre de Cristo Range, where steep alpine basins, loose talus, and exposed ridgelines define the standard route. Private land boundaries restrict southern and eastern approaches, making the Lake Como Road approach from the west the primary standard public route. Most climbers use this approach, which crosses montane forest and alpine tundra before reaching the summit in a zone of rapid weather transitions and significant wind exposure.
routeDescription: The Lake Como Road Route is the standard and most popular approach to Blanca Peak. Most parties begin from the lower Lake Como Road parking area near 8,000 feet, though high-clearance vehicles may continue to higher pullouts depending on road conditions and vehicle capability. The initial segment follows an extremely rough four-wheel-drive road with multiple switchbacks; passenger cars typically stop at the lower parking area, while high-clearance and four-wheel-drive vehicles may advance to successively higher parking points. Beyond the highest practical parking point, most parties continue on foot up Lake Como Road. The road reaches Lake Como at about 11,750 feet, continues around the lake, and ends near Blue Lakes around 12,200 feet. From there, climbers continue on trail and talus toward Blanca's northwest ridge, with the upper route involving steep Class 2 hiking and scrambling on loose rock, with route-finding required near the headwall and summit ridge. Total distance varies widely depending on parking location, ranging from approximately 14 to 17 miles round trip with roughly 6,000 to 6,500 feet of elevation gain. Most parties complete the climb in 8–12 hours, though low-start or overnight itineraries may take longer.
permitsClimbing: No permits are generally required for day hiking on National Forest land. Dispersed camping is available in the Lake Como area, but overnight rules, fire restrictions, group-size limits, and camping closures can change. Check with the Rio Grande National Forest before planning overnight trips.
permitsCamping: Lake Como serves as the primary overnight camping area for parties ascending Blanca. The lake sits at approximately 11,750 feet in a high alpine setting with no developed facilities; camping is dispersed. Camp well away from lakes, streams, and trails, and treat all water. Private land boundaries restrict southern and eastern access; use only established public access routes.
wildlife: July through September is the standard climbing window on the Lake Como route. The upper route involves Class 2 scrambling with route-finding in alpine terrain above treeline and comfort moving on loose rock and exposed high-elevation terrain. Afternoon thunderstorms are common in July and August; parties must plan for rapid weather deterioration on exposed ridge terrain with significant wind and lightning hazard during storms. Early-season snowfields, postholing, hidden holes, and icy slopes may persist near Lake Como and above the lakes. Loose rock on the final ridge and headwall requires careful foot placement, especially when multiple parties are on the mountain.
footnote: Summit 37.57753°N, 105.48566°W. Lake Como Road lower parking area (~8,000 ft). Elevation 14,351 ft; prominence 5,331 ft. Rio Grande National Forest.

overview: Mount Timpanogos ranks No. 47 among the 57 ultra-prominent peaks in the contiguous United States, rising 11,752 feet in the Wasatch Range of northern Utah with 5,270 feet of clean prominence. It is the second-highest summit in the Wasatch Range above Utah Valley. Most hikers use the Timpooneke Trail for 14 miles round-trip with 4,389 feet of gain on Class 2 terrain. Timed Timpooneke trailhead parking applies on summer weekends and holidays via Recreation.gov.

Timpanogos is a mountain of basins, cliffs, meadows, snowfields, and broken limestone. Two main trails reach the summit area, with Timpooneke generally serving as the standard route. The hike is not technical in normal summer conditions, but it is long, high, exposed, and popular. Strong fitness, an early start, and close attention to weather are the main requirements.

Mount Timpanogos is a Class 2 hike that requires strong fitness, comfort with altitude, and basic alpine judgment. The sustained elevation gain and summit elevation create a fitness-based challenge rather than a technical one. Route-finding is generally straightforward in normal summer conditions, though the final summit section requires comfort on rocky terrain and light scrambling. No ropes or climbing equipment are required for the standard summer route.
routeDescription: The Timpooneke Trail is the standard route, rising 14 miles round-trip with 4,389 feet of elevation gain. The route departs from Timpooneke Campground at about 7,370 feet, follows a well-marked path through alternating forest and open meadows, and gains elevation steadily toward the upper mountain.

Above the basin, the route climbs to Timpanogos Saddle at approximately 11,050 feet, a natural rest point with open views of Utah Valley and the surrounding peaks. The final 0.8 miles involve rocky Class 2 terrain and talus on the west side of the summit ridge, with about 700 feet of elevation gain. The route remains well-marked in normal summer conditions and does not require technical climbing. The main difficulty is the sustained gain, length, altitude, and exposure rather than any single technical crux.
routeLandmarks: Lower Basin: forest and meadow travel below the upper mountain
Upper Basin meadow: broad alpine basin below the saddle
Summit ridge: rocky Class 2 terrain with weather exposure
permitsClimbing: No climbing permit is required for the standard summer route. Mount Timpanogos is within the Mount Timpanogos Wilderness, managed by the Uinta-Wasatch-Cache National Forest. Timed parking permits are required for the Timpooneke trailhead on Saturdays, Sundays, and holidays during the summer season, including Pioneer Day in July and Labor Day in September. Permits are available through Recreation.gov. Each reservation covers one private vehicle for a designated time slot. Day-use requirements, release schedules, and fees may change; verify current rules with the Forest Service and Recreation.gov before traveling.
permitsCamping: Overnight backcountry camping does not require a separate wilderness permit. Maximum group size is 15 persons. Timpooneke Campground, located near the trailhead, provides reservable family campsites.

Emerald Lake sits below the summit massif at approximately 10,390 feet and is more commonly reached from the Aspen Grove trailhead (about 5.1 miles one-way with roughly 3,500 feet of elevation gain). It can also be accessed as a longer side trip from the Timpooneke route. It is one of the most popular overnight areas on the mountain, with established sites nearby and a historic stone shelter. Dispersed camping is permitted in the wilderness area, subject to local setback rules from water sources, trails, and fragile meadow habitat.
wildlife: Peak hiking season usually runs from late June through early October, depending on snowpack and early fall storms. The mountain can hold snow well into summer, especially in basins, gullies, and shaded north-facing terrain. July and August generally offer the most reliable trail access but also bring crowds and afternoon thunderstorms. September and early October often have cooler temperatures and fewer hikers, though early fall snow and storms become increasingly likely. Winter ascents are possible, including via Everest Ridge, but require mountaineering skills and avalanche awareness.

overview: Ibapah Peak ranks No. 49 among the 57 ultra-prominent peaks in the contiguous United States, rising 12,087 feet in the Deep Creek Mountains of far western Utah with 5,247 feet of clean prominence. It is the highest summit in the Deep Creek Range, where the Goshute Indian Reservation blocks western approaches and confines access to Granite Creek Canyon. Most hikers use the Granite Creek Road Approach for 14.9 miles round-trip with 5,990 feet of gain on Class 2 terrain.

The peak sits within remote BLM-managed terrain characterized by stark geographic isolation where self-sufficiency and self-rescue planning are essential. Granite architecture dominates the drainage, with impressive granite cliffs framing Granite Creek Canyon. Haystack Peak, nearly equal in elevation at 11,980+ feet, rises nearby and can be visited as an extension to the standard ascent.
routeDescription: The standard route ascends Ibapah via the Granite Creek drainage, starting from a trailhead accessed via rough dirt roads from the Tooele Valley. Parking elevation varies based on vehicle high-clearance capability: low clearance vehicles park around 6,900 feet, while high-clearance or 4WD vehicles can drive to approximately 7,500 feet. The climb gains 5,990 feet over 14.9 miles round-trip and typically takes 10 to 12 hours for a strenuous day hike, though a 2-day backpack with an overnight at higher elevation is a safer approach for many parties.

From the highest trailhead parking, the route follows a closed roadbed through the Granite Creek drainage, gaining elevation steadily through a transition zone from sagebrush desert to coniferous forest. Stream crossings are necessary—water is reliable through the forest zone. The terrain becomes increasingly steep, with few switchbacks and a sustained climb above 10,000 feet. At approximately 10,000 feet, the forest opens into a large alpine meadow. Route-finding becomes critical here: a faintly cairned trail crosses the meadow toward a distinct sub-peak at roughly 11,000 feet, but the trail is frustratingly difficult to follow on the ascent and requires care. Above the meadow, the route becomes off-trail, ascending toward the saddle between the sub-peak and Ibapah's summit. The final 900 vertical feet to the 12,087-foot summit follow a well-cairned, nontechnical ridge over mostly stable terrain.
permitsClimbing: No permits are required to climb Ibapah Peak. The area is managed by the Bureau of Land Management, and dispersed camping is free at the Granite Creek Trailhead and throughout the surrounding BLM lands. Fire rings exist at the trailhead area for established campsites. Climbers should remain on the Granite Creek approach and avoid crossing onto Goshute Indian Reservation lands without explicit permission. Because the approach is long and rough—40+ miles of dirt roads from the nearest paved highway—advance vehicle planning is essential. High-clearance or 4WD capability is strongly recommended. Parties should arrive with full water containers and self-sufficient camping supplies; no facilities exist near the trailhead.
permitsCamping: For overnight parties, camping options exist between 7,500 feet (near the road access) and approximately 10,000 feet in the alpine meadow zone. Water availability decreases significantly above 10,000 feet; reliable water sources are present in stream drainages through the forested section and are marginal in the upper meadow. Although Granite Creek usually provides water through the forested drainage, parties should not depend on untreated or seasonal sources and should arrive with enough carrying capacity for long dry stretches. Standard BLM dispersed camping practices apply, but visitors should confirm current restrictions, especially fire rules and any temporary closures, before driving in.
wildlife: May through October represents the optimal climbing window. June through September is peak season, when snow has cleared from the meadow and water is most reliable. Late May and early June require preparation for residual snowpack that may block the upper meadow approaches. September and early October offer stable weather and lower temperatures but require attention to early snow possibility and shorter daylight hours. Map-and-compass or GPS navigation is essential through the faint cairned trail in the alpine meadow and off-trail sections above 10,000 feet. Afternoon thunderstorms are possible during climbing season, and nonexistent cell service throughout the approach demands complete self-sufficiency.
footnote: Summit 39.794°N, 113.458°W. Granite Creek Trailhead N 39.851° W 113.385° (~6,900–7,500 ft). Elevation 12,087 ft; prominence 5,247 ft. BLM-managed lands.

overview: Lassen Peak ranks No. 50 among the 57 ultra-prominent peaks in the contiguous United States, rising 10,457 feet in the Cascade Range of northeastern California, with 5,246 feet of clean prominence. It is the highest point in Lassen Volcanic National Park and the southernmost active volcano in the Cascade Range. Most hikers use the Lassen Peak Trail for 5 miles round-trip with 2,000 feet of gain on Class 1 terrain.

Lassen Peak is commonly cited by the National Park Service as the world's largest volcanic dome. Its eruption sequence between 1914 and 1917 was the most recent Cascade Range eruption before Mount Saint Helens in 1980. Lassen Volcanic National Park remains geothermally active, with fumaroles, hot springs, and mud pots visible in hydrothermal areas throughout the park.

The standard Lassen Peak Trail requires only Class 1 hiking but gains roughly 2,000 feet in 2.5 miles across steep, exposed volcanic terrain. The route is well-maintained and straightforward, making the summit accessible to experienced day hikers while still demanding respect for elevation, sun exposure, wind, lingering snow, and rapid weather changes typical of high Cascade peaks.

Lassen Peak's volcanic dome is roughly 11,000 feet wide and 2,000 feet high, making it one of the defining features of the southern Cascade Range. The Lassen Peak Trail is one of the park's most heavily used summit hikes, particularly during July and August. Visits in June or September typically offer lighter crowds, while October can be quiet but brings higher risk of snow, ice, road closures, and storms.
routeDescription: The Lassen Peak Trail begins at the Lassen Peak Trailhead at approximately 8,500 feet on the park highway. The route covers 5 miles round-trip with about 2,000 feet of elevation gain, typically requiring 3 to 5 hours. The trail climbs through long switchbacks on volcanic cinder and pumice, then follows an exposed ridge to the summit. The path is well-maintained Class 1 hiking throughout, with no technical scrambling required in normal summer conditions.

Early-season hikers may encounter lingering snow and ice on the upper trail, particularly in June and into July after heavy winters. Microspikes or crampons and an ice axe may be useful when snow covers the route. The upper mountain is fully exposed to sun, wind, and lightning; an alpine start is standard practice to clear the ridge before afternoon thunderstorms develop.
routeLandmarks: Lassen Peak Trailhead (8,500 ft): parking, restrooms, seasonal information
Lassen Peak Summit (10,457 ft): panoramic views
permitsClimbing: A park entrance pass is required to access Lassen Volcanic National Park. No separate climbing permit is required for day hiking the Lassen Peak Trail. Entrance passes can be purchased at park entrance stations or online through Recreation.gov. America the Beautiful and other federal recreation passes are accepted in lieu of the standard entrance fee. Verify current fee amounts and pass requirements on the Lassen Volcanic National Park website or Recreation.gov before departure.
permitsCamping: Developed campgrounds within Lassen Volcanic National Park include Manzanita Lake, Summit Lake, Southwest Walk-In, and others. Campground reservations are available through Recreation.gov and are strongly recommended during peak summer months. Park roads and campgrounds operate seasonally; the Lassen Park Highway (CA-89) typically closes in winter due to snow. Check current road, campground, and trail status with the park before planning overnight trips.
wildlife: June through September is the standard climbing window for the Lassen Peak Trail, though exact opening dates depend on snowpack and park road access. Afternoon thunderstorms are common in summer; the exposed upper ridge offers no shelter from lightning. Wildfire smoke can affect air quality and visibility from July through October. Elsewhere in the park, hydrothermal areas present burn and injury hazards from boiling water, steam, and unstable ground; stay on designated boardwalks and trails in thermal zones. Volcanic rock on the summit trail can be loose and abrasive; sturdy footwear and trekking poles help on the cinder and pumice sections.
footnote: Sources: Willhite Web | Lassen Volcanic NPS | USGS | Recreation.gov

overview: Mount Cleveland ranks No. 48 among the 57 ultra-prominent peaks in the contiguous United States, rising 10,466 feet in the Lewis Range of Glacier National Park, Montana, with 5,252 feet of clean prominence. It is the highest summit in Glacier National Park on the Continental Divide near the Canadian border. Most parties use the Stoney Indian Route for roughly 28 to 32 miles round-trip with about 6,300 to 6,700 feet of gain on Class 4 terrain. Backcountry permits with advance Recreation.gov reservations are required for overnight itineraries.

Climbers approaching Mount Cleveland face one of the more logistically demanding ultra-prominent peaks in the Lower 48. International border proximity, limited backcountry permit availability, long wilderness approaches, and complex route-finding make this a serious objective for experienced and self-sufficient parties. Mount Cleveland is also associated with one of Glacier National Park's most serious mountaineering tragedies: a December 1969 avalanche on the west face killed five climbers.
routeDescription: The standard approach to Mount Cleveland follows the Stoney Indian Route via Stoney Indian Lake, roughly 28–32 miles round-trip depending on approach, with over 6,500 feet of cumulative elevation gain. Most parties complete the climb in 2 to 3 days. Parties commonly access the route via the Goat Haunt area, which may involve a Waterton Lake boat shuttle from Canada, or by approaching from the Kootenay Lake area. Both approaches converge near Stoney Indian Lake, the primary base camp for the route. From Stoney Indian Lake, the route traverses the exposed east side of the Stoney Indian Peaks before ascending the 3rd and 4th class summit terrain on loose scree, talus, and blocky rock. The traverse is narrow and exposed, with little margin for error. Route-finding difficulties are commonly reported, especially in poor visibility. The descent is punishing on loose terrain and requires careful navigation.

Other established approaches include the West Face Route, a serious alternative with significant avalanche exposure, the Southeast Face Route from Chief Mountain Customs via Glenns Lake and Whitecrow Glacier, and longer U.S.-based options via the Belly River Trail from Chief Mountain Customs, the Highline Trail from Logan Pass, or Many Glacier via Ptarmigan Tunnel. These alternatives generally require more time, stronger route-finding ability, and greater technical judgment than the Stoney Indian Route.
routeLandmarks: Stoney Indian Peaks east traverse, approximately 9,500–10,000 ft
permitsClimbing: Backcountry permits are required for overnight camping in Glacier National Park. Mount Cleveland is usually approached through highly competitive backcountry zones, and advance reservations through Recreation.gov are strongly recommended. Limited walk-up availability may exist, but parties should not rely on obtaining a same-day permit for this objective. Glacier National Park charges backcountry permit and per-person camping fees during the main season. Fee amounts, reservation windows, and walk-up procedures can change, so parties should confirm current requirements with Glacier National Park and Recreation.gov before planning an itinerary. Commercial guiding may be restricted and requires proper authorization. Climbers should assume they need to be self-sufficient unless they have confirmed access to a legally authorized guide service.
permitsCamping: Stoney Indian Lake is the designated backcountry campsite closest to the peak, but availability is limited. Parties unable to secure Stoney Indian Lake may need to plan alternative camps at Kootenay Lake, Goat Haunt, or other permitted backcountry sites. All parties must follow Glacier National Park food-storage regulations. Approved bear-resistant storage or designated food-storage infrastructure may be required depending on the campsite. Bear spray is strongly recommended and should be readily accessible. Campfire restrictions, group-size limits, and distance-from-water regulations apply; confirm current rules with park rangers when obtaining permits.
wildlife: The Stoney Indian Route involves sustained scrambling on unstable scree and talus, exposed traversing with little margin for error, and complex route-finding on and around the Stoney Indian Peaks. The summit terrain includes 3rd and 4th class scrambling on loose rock. Roped climbing is not typically required for experienced parties in dry summer conditions, but exposure, loose rock, and route-finding make the route serious. Route descriptions from experienced parties consistently mention disorientation on the exposed traverse and difficulty relocating the descent route under stress, fatigue, or poor visibility. Mount Cleveland sits in a severe alpine environment where weather can deteriorate quickly, even in midsummer. Early-season attempts, especially in June and early July, may encounter heavy snowpack on narrow ledges, creating avalanche danger and serious slip risk. Late-season attempts face shorter days, colder temperatures, and early winter storms. The best climbing window is usually late July through mid-September, when snow coverage is lower and weather is generally more stable. Winter and early-spring ascents are serious mountaineering objectives requiring advanced snow, avalanche, and cold-weather skills.
footnote: Elevation 10,466 ft (commonly cited; LiDAR-derived sources may vary slightly). Prominence approximately 5,252 ft (ListsOfJohn). Route mileage varies by approach (Goat Haunt, Belly River, Kootenai/Kintla). Confirm current Glacier NP backcountry permit windows, fees, and access procedures on NPS/Recreation.gov before travel. International travel logistics and Canadian border proximity may affect Waterton access.

overview: Arc Dome ranks No. 51 among the 57 ultra-prominent peaks in the contiguous United States, rising 11,778 feet in the Toiyabe Range of central Nevada with 5,213 feet of clean prominence. It is the highest summit in the Toiyabe Range within the 120,551-acre Arc Dome Wilderness Area. Most hikers use the Columbine Campground Route for 14.6 miles round-trip with 4,335 feet of gain on Class 1 terrain.

The range's high elevation and exposed crest hold lingering snow into the shoulder seasons, creating a cooler high-country environment above the surrounding desert basins. From the summit, visibility extends across the central Great Basin and into California, including views of Mount Jefferson, the White Mountains, and surrounding ranges.
routeDescription: The standard route begins at Columbine Campground, approximately 8,600 feet, and follows the Columbine Trail toward Stewart Creek before reaching the Toiyabe Crest National Recreation Trail. The route climbs roughly 4,335 feet over 14.6 miles round-trip. Near the upper basin, the route turns toward Arc Dome and climbs through open meadows with broad views of the southwest face. The grade steepens near the saddle at the head of the canyon, where tight switchbacks lead toward the summit ridge. Above 11,000 feet, the trail crosses talus and loose rock, making progress slower despite the nontechnical terrain. The final section remains Class 1 hiking on an established trail.

Columbine Campground is remote, and the approach can take longer than mileage alone suggests. From US 50 west of Austin, Nevada, take State Route 722 and Route 21 via the Yomba Indian Reservation, then follow Forest Service Road 119 for approximately 10 miles to the campground. The final stretch is rough dirt road; high-clearance vehicles are recommended, and conditions can vary seasonally. Plan extra time for the drive and verify current road conditions with the Humboldt-Toiyabe National Forest before departing.
routeLandmarks: Summit ridge and Arc Dome (11,778 ft)
permitsClimbing: No climbing permit or fee is required for Arc Dome. Normal wilderness regulations apply to backcountry camping. The Arc Dome Wilderness Area prohibits motorized vehicles and mechanized transport, including bicycles.
permitsCamping: Columbine Campground trailhead offers free campsites with 7 camp units and functions as the primary access point. The campground may see heavier use during hunting season. Backcountry camping is possible along the route where durable sites and water access allow, but the Toiyabe Crest is exposed and often dry. Camp at least 100 feet from water sources. Fires may be restricted, especially near or above tree line and during dry periods; check current Forest Service regulations before your trip.
wildlife: The route is strenuous Class 1 hiking on established trails and loose upper-mountain tread. The crux follows tight switchbacks across loose rock and talus above 11,000 feet; while the route remains Class 1, the steep grade, unstable footing, and elevation make the final 1,000 vertical feet the most strenuous part of the climb. June through September offers the most reliable conditions. May and October are possible depending on snowpack and weather. Early-season and late-season ascents may require waterproof boots and gaiters due to lingering snow and wet conditions above 10,000 feet. Summer temperatures at the trailhead can be hot, and afternoon thunderstorms are frequent; parties should start early and aim to be off exposed high terrain before storms develop. The Toiyabe Crest has little reliable water; carry sufficient capacity and plan water sources carefully. Wild horses may be encountered in the Toiyabe Range and Arc Dome Wilderness, particularly near open meadows and along portions of the Toiyabe Crest Trail. Mule deer and sage grouse may also be seen. Treat or filter all backcountry water due to wildlife and grazing activity in the area.
footnote: Elevation commonly cited as 11,778 ft; sources vary between 11,773 and 11,778 ft. Prominence 5,213 ft (ListsOfJohn). Trailhead: Columbine Campground N 38.90009° W 117.37609°. Route stats per Humboldt-Toiyabe National Forest and standard trip reports (14.6 miles round-trip, 4,335 ft gain).

overview: She-Devil ranks No. 52 among the 57 ultra-prominent peaks in the contiguous United States, rising 9,400 feet in the Seven Devils Mountains of western Idaho near Hells Canyon with 5,200 feet of clean prominence. It is the high point of a rugged volcanic massif above one of North America's deepest river gorges on the Idaho-Oregon border. Most climbers use the She-Devil High Route for 7 or more miles round-trip with 3,000 to 4,000 feet of gain on Class 3 terrain across loose volcanic rock.

The high-point status of She-Devil versus He-Devil was historically ambiguous because the two summits are nearly identical in height. Recent precision survey data has measured She-Devil at 9,385.89 feet and He-Devil at 9,385.63 feet, suggesting that She-Devil is higher by approximately three inches. This makes She-Devil the currently accepted ultra-prominent peak of the pair and the recognized target for climbers pursuing the Ultra-57 list. Older publications and some sources may still list the peaks as co-equal or attribute ultra-prominent status to He-Devil. Parties pursuing the list can plan a direct ascent of She-Devil alone via Class 3 terrain, avoiding the Class 4 complications that arise when attempting to link both peaks via the direct ridge traverse. Many climbers nonetheless visit both peaks in a single day by descending She-Devil and approaching He-Devil separately, though this adds significant time and elevation loss.
routeDescription: The She-Devil High Route approaches from Windy Saddle Trailhead, accessed via Forest Road 517 from Riggins. From the trailhead at roughly 7,600 feet, the route joins the Seven Devils Loop Trail junction and transitions to cross-country terrain toward the Sheep Lake area. The route involves approximately 3,000 to 4,000 feet of cumulative elevation gain due to ridge transitions, lake approaches, and multiple ascents across complex terrain. The overall commitment typically requires 7 to 10 hours. Class 3 scrambling dominates the High Route's ridge approaches, with terrain composed of exposed talus and scree fields throughout. With careful route-finding, the standard direct ascent of She-Devil stays solidly Class 3. The Class 4 terrain is encountered only when attempting a direct ridge traverse from She-Devil to He-Devil, a connection that requires experienced scrambling and significant exposure. Most parties who want both peaks instead descend She-Devil and approach He-Devil separately via its standard northwest ridge (Class 2–3), which adds considerable time and elevation loss but avoids the difficult traverse.
permitsClimbing: No standard recreation permit is typically required for day use or overnight travel in Hells Canyon Wilderness, but visitors should check current Forest Service regulations before departure.
permitsCamping: Dispersed camping is generally permitted throughout the wilderness, including near Windy Saddle and surrounding backcountry areas, though facilities are minimal and parties should be fully self-sufficient with water, food, and waste management. Camp at least 100 feet from water sources where possible. Fire restrictions, group-size limits, and seasonal closures can change, especially during late summer wildfire season.
wildlife: July through September is peak season for the She-Devil High Route, when snow has cleared from the alpine terrain and access via Forest Road 517 is generally reliable. Loose volcanic rock and extensive talus and scree fields dominate the route, with dead-ends, loose gullies, and vanishing ledges possible when route-finding fails. Exposed positions carry wind exposure and rapid weather changes; afternoon thunderstorms are possible in summer. The remote Hells Canyon Wilderness location demands complete self-sufficiency and self-rescue capability.
footnote: Summit 45.5600°N, 116.3750°W. Windy Saddle Trailhead N 45.5622° W 116.3783° (~7,600 ft). Elevation 9,400 ft; prominence 5,200 ft. Hells Canyon Wilderness, Nez Perce-Clearwater National Forest.

overview: Abercrombie Mountain ranks No. 53 among the 57 ultra-prominent peaks in the contiguous United States, rising 7,310 feet in the Selkirk Mountains of northeastern Washington, with 5,174 feet of clean prominence. It is the highest point in Stevens County and often cited as the second-highest peak in eastern Washington. Most hikers use Flume Creek Trail #502 for 8 miles round-trip with 2,300 feet of gain on Class 1–2 terrain.

Although modest by Cascade standards, Abercrombie towers above the Pend Oreille River valley and the surrounding Colville National Forest in one of Washington's quieter and more isolated mountain settings.

Named for Lieutenant William R. Abercrombie, who explored the nearby Pend Oreille River in 1879 and 1883, the mountain served as the site of a fire lookout tower from 1934 until its demolition in the 1960s. Stone steps and a small stone shelter remain at the summit along with a peak register.
routeDescription: The standard eastern approach follows Flume Creek Trail #502 from the Flume Creek Trailhead at 5,120 feet. The route covers 8 miles round-trip with 2,300 feet of gain over moderate grades, climbing roughly 3.9 miles one way through forested terrain with several creek crossings. Dry campsites lie along the trail at 1.9 and 3.1 miles for parties making an overnight trip.

At 3.1 miles and approximately 6,700 feet, the trail reaches a junction with Abercrombie Trail; bear right toward the summit. The final 0.8 mile steepens considerably over loose talus and scree to the open summit plateau. Most parties complete the round-trip in 4 to 6 hours.
routeLandmarks: Flume Creek Trailhead (5,120 ft) — limited parking; accessed via FR 6200350 / Flume Creek Road from Boundary Road north of Metaline Falls
Campsite areas (1.9 miles and 3.1 miles) — dry campsites
Trail junction with Abercrombie Trail (3.1 miles, ~6,700 ft) — bear right
Summit plateau (7,310 ft) — fire lookout remnants, peak register
permitsClimbing: No permit is required to climb Abercrombie Mountain. The route lies on Colville National Forest land with no fee, self-registration, or quota for day use.
permitsCamping: Two dry campsites lie along the standard route at 1.9 and 3.1 miles. Dispersed camping is allowed elsewhere on National Forest land subject to current regulations. Bear-resistant food storage is required; use an IGBC-approved container or approved hang method. Pack out all waste.
wildlife: No specialized technical skills are required beyond standard hiking and Class 1–2 scrambling on the final talus section. Grizzly bears inhabit the broader Selkirk ecosystem; store food securely and keep a clean camp. Mosquitoes can be intense from June through August. Afternoon thunderstorms develop rapidly over exposed alpine terrain in mid-summer. The final 0.8 mile crosses loose talus and scree. Forest Road 6200350 / Flume Creek Road to the trailhead is rough and may require high-clearance vehicles. The peak sits in a remote corner of northeastern Washington with limited services nearby.
footnote: Sources: Colville National Forest Recreation | Willhite Web | Out There Outdoors | Peakbagger | SummitPost | WTA

overview: Mount Lemmon ranks No. 55 among the 57 ultra-prominent peaks in the contiguous United States, rising 9,171 feet in the Santa Catalina Mountains north of Tucson, Arizona, with 5,164 feet of clean prominence. It is the highest summit in the Santa Catalina Mountains and Pima County. Most hikers use the Aspen Trail and Marshall Gulch Trail from Marshall Gulch for roughly 4 to 9 miles round-trip with about 2,000 to 3,500 feet of gain on Class 1 terrain. A Coronado National Forest or Interagency pass is required to park at Marshall Gulch.

The peak carries the O'odham name Babad Do'ag, meaning Frog Mountain, and was later named for botanist Sara Plummer Lemmon, who reached the summit on her 1881 honeymoon. The Catalina Highway climbs roughly 27 miles from the desert edge to the summit zone, gaining more than 6,500 feet in the process, an elevation change that compresses the ecological equivalent of driving from Mexico to Canada into a single mountain corridor.

The summit area supports mixed conifer and fir forest entirely unlike the surrounding Sonoran Desert. This abrupt transition, combined with 5,164 feet of prominence, makes the peak a significant ecological island above the desert floor. The summit zone includes the Lemmon Rock Lookout fire tower and the village of Summerhaven. Mount Lemmon Ski Valley, on the north face near the summit, is widely noted as the southernmost ski area in the continental United States.
routeDescription: The primary foot approaches to the summit use the Aspen Trail and the Marshall Gulch Trail, both of which begin at Marshall Gulch Picnic Area at the end of the road south of Summerhaven. The two trails are commonly combined as a loop of approximately 3.9 miles with around 1,500 feet of gain. Parties seeking the true summit can continue from Marshall Saddle via the Aspen Trail's upper section toward the summit zone, adding mileage and gain; because roads reach the upper mountain, total hiking distance and elevation gain vary considerably depending on chosen trailhead and finishing point. The terrain is maintained trail throughout, remaining Class 1 with no scrambling required. The Lemmon Rock Lookout Trail provides an alternate approach from a different point along the Catalina Highway.
routeLandmarks: Marshall Gulch Picnic Area (standard trailhead south of Summerhaven)
Marshall Saddle (junction for summit extension via upper Aspen Trail)
Lemmon Rock Lookout Trail (alternate Catalina Highway approach)
permitsClimbing: No hiking permit or trail quota applies on Mount Lemmon or in the surrounding Santa Catalinas within Coronado National Forest. Parking at Marshall Gulch Picnic Area requires a Coronado National Forest or Interagency pass; day-use passes are available on-site for $8 per vehicle. Marshall Gulch is a day-use area; overnight parking for backpackers is permitted at the trailhead only, and picnicking overnight is not permitted. Confirm current fee rules and any seasonal gate closures with the Santa Catalina Ranger District before your trip.
permitsCamping: Dispersed camping is available on Coronado National Forest land in the Santa Catalinas at no charge, subject to current fire restrictions and camping regulations. The upper Catalina Highway gate is closed seasonally from December 15 through February 28, restricting vehicle access to the Marshall Gulch area during that period. Fire restrictions are common and can be strict in this region; check current conditions with the Santa Catalina Ranger District before any overnight trip.
wildlife: Mount Lemmon is Class 1 on maintained trails for all standard foot routes. No technical gear or scrambling skills are required under normal conditions. The primary hazards are weather: summer thunderstorms can develop quickly above the ridgeline between July and September, and winter can bring snow, ice, road closures, and hazardous footing at upper elevations. Wind exposure near the summit is significant year-round. The mountain's southerly latitude means winter snow melts faster than on ranges farther north, but upper trails can remain icy or snow-covered from late fall through early spring.
footnote: Summit N 32.44330° W 110.78820°. Marshall Gulch Picnic Area: N 32.42806° W 110.75528°. Elevation 9,171 ft; prominence 5,164 ft. Coronado National Forest, Santa Catalina Mountains.

overview: Chiricahua Peak ranks No. 54 among the 57 ultra-prominent peaks in the contiguous United States, rising 9,776 feet in the Chiricahua Mountains of southeastern Arizona with 5,163 feet of clean prominence. It is the highest summit in the Chiricahua Wilderness within Coronado National Forest. Most hikers use the Crest Trail from Rustler Park for 10.9 miles round-trip with 2,152 feet of gain on Class 1 terrain. A day-use fee may apply at the Rustler Park trailhead.

The name Chiricahua is commonly associated with Indigenous-language references to wild turkey, with interpretations including "Chiwi Kawi" or "Turkey Mountain." The name holds cultural significance to the Chiricahua Apache and other tribes historically connected to this region. The area is adjacent to Chiricahua National Monument to the north, famous for its distinctive hoodoo rock formations. Chiricahua Peak remains a relatively uncrowded destination despite its prominence and the quality of surrounding wilderness experience.
routeDescription: The standard route ascends from Rustler Park via the Crest Trail, beginning at 8,400 feet and winding along high-elevation ridges to the summit. Most parties complete the ascent in 10.9 miles round-trip with 2,152 feet of elevation gain in 5.5 to 6 hours. The trail traverses forested terrain and opens into high-elevation meadows before a final forested push to the summit. The ascent presents no technical difficulty and reads as a straightforward ridge walk. Some lower sections may show overgrowth, and loose rock on the final summit approach demands attention but poses no major crux. Longer variants combining the Crest Trail with other wilderness routes can extend the outing to 15 miles.

The Mormon Ridge Trail from Sycamore Campground provides a shorter western approach. This route begins at 6,270 feet and follows Mormon Ridge Trail #269 for 3.6 miles to Chiricahua Saddle, then connects to the Crest Trail for 0.7 miles to Junction Saddle and the final 0.5 miles to the summit. Round-trip distance is approximately 11 miles with steeper terrain than the Rustler Park route. The Mormon Canyon Trail offers an additional variant from the same trailhead, though it features significant elevation gain and considerable fire damage recovery in places. Both western routes provide solitude compared to the Rustler Park approach.
routeLandmarks: Rustler Park Trailhead (8,400 ft, standard start)
permitsClimbing: No permit is required for day hiking. An $8 day-use fee has been reported at Rustler Park Campground, payable by cash envelope at the entrance; federal passes may be accepted. Verify current status before travel. Self-registration may be available at the trailhead or wilderness access point.
permitsCamping: Free dispersed camping is abundant throughout the area. Developed campgrounds near the peak include Rustler Park (typically closed October–April), and nearby Chiricahua-area options such as Sycamore, Herb Martyr, and Rucker campgrounds, all managed by the Forest Service.
Rustler Park Campground notes black bears in the area; bear-aware food storage and proper use of provided bear boxes are essential for overnight trips.
wildlife: The optimal climbing season runs from May through September. Rustler Park campground may close seasonally in fall and is generally unavailable through winter; confirm current road and campground status before travel. Dense brush and overgrown terrain make off-trail travel unpleasant and should be avoided. Water supplies are undependable; confirm sources before relying on off-trail flow.
footnote: Summit N 31.84589° W 109.29130°. Rustler Park Trailhead: N 31.9052778° W 109.2794444° (8,400 ft). Elevation 9,776 ft; prominence 5,163 ft. Chiricahua Wilderness, Coronado National Forest.

overview: Mount Eddy ranks No. 56 among the 57 ultra-prominent peaks in the contiguous United States, rising 9,025 feet in the Trinity Divide of the Klamath Mountains in northern California, with 5,153 feet of clean prominence. It is the highest summit in the Trinity Divide and commonly cited as the highest peak in the United States west of Interstate 5. Most hikers use the Parks Creek Trail for 9.5 to 10.5 miles round-trip with 2,150 to 2,500 feet of gain on Class 1 terrain. Self-issue wilderness permits are required for overnight camping.

The peak's significance lies not in technical challenge but in its distinctiveness as a remote alpine summit accessible via high-altitude lakes and maintained trails. Surrounding the peak are several alpine lakes of exceptional clarity, meadows that support rare botanical species including carnivorous pitcher plants, and the historic route of the Sisson-Callahan National Recreation Trail, established in the 1880s to link ranger stations across the Trinity Divide.
routeDescription: The standard Parks Creek route gains approximately 2,150 to 2,500 feet over 9.5 to 10.5 miles round-trip, typically requiring 5 to 7 hours. The route begins at Parks Creek Summit Trailhead at 6,800 feet and follows the Pacific Crest Trail southbound across nearly flat terrain to Deadfall Meadows at approximately 7,400 feet. From there, a well-signed junction leads to the Deadfall Lakes basin, where the trail climbs steadily through alpine terrain to Upper Deadfall Lake at approximately 7,800 feet. From the upper lakes, the route ascends steep, rocky switchbacks toward Deadfall Summit (approximately 9,000 feet) and the summit ridge before the short final approach to Mount Eddy's broad summit. The upper switchbacks are rocky and loose in places, requiring careful foot placement but no scrambling.
permitsClimbing: No wilderness permit is required for day hiking. Overnight camping at Deadfall Lakes requires a free self-issue wilderness permit available at Parks Creek Trailhead or by contacting Mount Shasta Ranger Station at (530) 926-4511. A California campfire permit is required year-round if using a stove or campfire. Campfire permits are free and available online at permit.preventwildfiresca.org or at any Forest Service ranger station.
permitsCamping: The Deadfall Lakes basin has commonly used backcountry campsites, particularly near Middle Deadfall Lake. Upper Deadfall Lake, while popular, sits in a fragile alpine meadow that is exposed to wind and requires careful site selection to minimize impact.
