Avoid camping in or adjacent to meadows and practice low-impact techniques. Water is abundant from alpine lakes. Parks Creek Summit Trailhead has a map kiosk and vault toilet; verify current facility status with the ranger station. Road access to Parks Creek is seasonal, with typical access from mid-June through mid-October; early-season and late-season snow can block Forest Road 17.
wildlife: June through October is the standard climbing window, with typical road access from mid-June through mid-October. The route is Class 1 hiking throughout with no scrambling, rope work, or specialized mountain skills required. Trekking poles are strongly recommended for boggy meadow sections and loose upper switchbacks. Early-season ascents in June may encounter significant snow above 8,500 feet.
footnote: Summit 41.3207°N, 122.4544°W. Parks Creek Summit Trailhead N 41.31968° W 122.47927° (~6,800 ft). Elevation 9,025 ft; prominence 5,153 ft. Shasta-Trinity National Forest.

overview: Miller Peak ranks No. 57 among the 57 ultra-prominent peaks in the contiguous United States, rising 9,468 feet in the Huachuca Mountains of southeastern Arizona with 5,007 feet of clean prominence. It is the highest summit in the Huachuca Mountains within Miller Peak Wilderness near the Mexico border. Most hikers use the Huachuca Crest Trail from Montezuma Pass for 11 miles round-trip with 3,150 feet of gain on Class 1 terrain.

The Huachuca Mountains support exceptional biodiversity, with over 170 bird species recorded in the region, including 14 species of hummingbirds that visit higher elevations during breeding season. The 2011 Monument Fire significantly altered the summit ecosystem, burning extensive pine and fir forest and leaving a landscape visibly shaped by post-fire erosion and recovery under mixed oak scrub. The peak remains a relatively understated destination for serious peak-baggers, offering solitude and the distinctive context of Arizona's borderland geography.
routeDescription: A common standard route ascends from Montezuma Pass in Coronado National Memorial via the Huachuca Crest Trail, beginning at 6,559 feet and climbing consistently along high-country ridgeline terrain to the summit. The route gains approximately 3,150 feet in 11 miles round-trip, requiring approximately 8 hours for most parties. The initial section climbs steeply through mixed pine and oak forest, with significant elevation gain early in the hike. The route follows the Huachuca Crest Trail along high-country ridgeline terrain toward the summit, with loose rock in places but no technical climbing. The Arizona Birding Trail notes the route is steep, rugged, and locally affected by post-fire erosion; despite that history, the trail is generally maintained and remains the standard approach.
routeLandmarks: Huachuca Crest Trail junction (high-elevation ridgeline, route backbone)
Summit approach (loose rock sections, forested final push)
permitsClimbing: No permits are required for day hiking Miller Peak.
permitsCamping: The Coronado National Forest allows dispersed camping in many undeveloped areas, subject to posted closures, wilderness rules, fire restrictions, and Leave No Trace practices. Ramsey Vista Campground is a developed option nearby with minimal facilities. Water sources along the Crest Trail route are limited; verify availability before departure. Parties planning overnight trips must arrange water strategy carefully or plan resupply logistics well in advance.
wildlife: Best conditions are usually fall through spring, though late spring can be hot at the trailhead and winter may bring snow or ice near the summit. The combination of remoteness, limited water, major elevation gain, and variable upper-elevation weather makes early starts, conservative pacing, and adequate daylight margins important.
footnote: Summit N 31.39277° W 110.29285°. Montezuma Pass Trailhead, Coronado National Memorial: N 31.35036° W 110.28535° (6,559 ft). Elevation 9,468 ft; prominence 5,007 ft. Miller Peak Wilderness, Coronado National Forest.
